summary: Research-and-design workflow for A8 v2 sysfs surface (replace failing dev_groups) and Test B-prime execution plan (persistence-engaged rmmod variant)
logs: Survey done. A3 pattern: captured. A8 v1 failure: A8 v1's `.driver.dev_groups = nv_tb_egpu_dev_groups` assignment is silently null. Sysfs reco: Use `sysfs_create_group(&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj, &nv_tb_egpu_attr_group)` in nvi.

Designs proposed: A — CONSERVATIVE: a near-verbatim clone of A3's (nv-tb-egpu-recover) / A2's (nv-tb-egpu-qwd) sysfs idiom. A8 v2 carries forward v1's atomic state struct, the 5 DEVICE_ATTR show functions, the NULL-terminated attribute array, and the file-static const attribute_group UNCHANGED; it only swaps the registration mechanism from the broken `pci_driver.driver.dev_groups` field-assignment to explicit `sysfs_create_group`/`sysfs_remove_group` calls invoked from new `tb_egpu_metrics_init`/`tb_egpu_metrics_stop` lifecycle helpers wired into nv_pci_probe / nv_pci_remove_helper alongside A2's QWD calls. Reviewer can lay A3's nv-tb-egpu-recover.c:786-884 next to A8 v2's metrics file and see the same skeleton line-for-line., Variant B — IMPROVED. Same surface contract as Variant A (5 read-only per-PCI-device attrs: tb_egpu_state, tb_egpu_f40b_fires, tb_egpu_recovery_count, tb_egpu_recovery_failures, tb_egpu_last_recovery_ns; same A9 hook symbols nv_tb_egpu_f40b_fired/recovery_succeeded/recovery_failed; same A6 wiring at the timeout path), but the failing pci_driver.driver.dev_groups registration is replaced with A3's proven explicit sysfs_create_group-at-probe / sysfs_remove_group-at-remove pattern, and the implementation is restructured for cleaner separation of concerns and better error propagation. Two new helpers split "register the attribute group" from "initialise the counter/state storage" so test code (and A9) can drive each independently. dev_groups was empirically falsified on this driver (A7-test-A-validation-2026-05-29.md line 163: symbols present in kallsyms but zero sysfs paths materialised), so Variant B is the only one that actually produces the surface the contract promises., Variant C — SHARED-PRIMITIVE. Factor a tiny `tb_egpu_sysfs_register()` / `tb_egpu_sysfs_unregister()` pair into the EXISTING A1-owned shared TU (`kernel-open/nvidia/nv-tb-egpu-pcie.{c,h}`), and convert A2's qwd, A3's recover, AND A8 v2's f40b surfaces to all call it. VERDICT: worth it, but ONLY because a shared tb-egpu primitive file (A1's nv-tb-egpu-pcie.c) already exists and is already consumed by A2+A3 — so the indirection adds a function, not a new file or a new ownership relationship. If A1 did NOT already own a shared TU, I would judge this premature and recommend Variant A (A8 v2 inlines the A3/A2 sysfs_create_group pattern, no refactor). Net is borderline-positive, NOT a slam dunk; see cons.

Judges done. Winners: CORRECTNESS — would each design actually materialise the 5 sysfs attributes at /sys/bus/pci/devices/<DBDF>/, free of races, NULL hazards, and lifecycle (attr-before-kobj / attr-after-free / unbind-rebind) bugs? Verified against the live A3 (nv-tb-egpu-recover.c:766-884) and A2 (nv-tb-egpu-qwd.c:350-455) idioms, the nv-pci.c probe/remove call sites (drvdata @1993, A3 init @2008, rm_init_private_state/is_external_gpu @2039, QWD init @2305, QWD stop @2387, A3 stop @2394), the nv_linux_state_t fields (nv-linux.h:1255/1445/1453), and the dev_groups clobber in the kernel itself (linux-v6.19/drivers/pci/pci-driver.c:1442 `drv->driver.dev_groups = drv->dev_groups;`).=B — IMPROVED, ROBUSTNESS — failure behavior of sysfs_create_group failure, partial/per-group failure, memory pressure, and concurrent bind/unbind. Grounded against the live tree: A1's nv-tb-egpu-pcie.{c,h} exists (verified, no sysfs code today), A2/A3 use sysfs_create_group on &nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj with NV_DBG_INFO log-and-continue and remove-first-then-free ordering (verified at recover.c:830-883 / qwd.c:406-454), no explicit locking in the addons (device_lock from driver core serializes the whole probe/remove callback), and A8's backing state is module-global single-GPU in all three variants. Key kernel-semantics facts that bound the analysis: (1) sysfs_create_group is atomic-on-failure for a SINGLE group — internal_create_group unwinds already-created attrs and returns an error, so 'some attrs created, some not' is NOT a reachable end-state within one group; partial failure only exists at the per-GROUP granularity (A3 group succeeds, A8 group fails), which all three handle identically and non-fatally. (2) kernfs active-reference draining in sysfs_remove_group guarantees an in-flight show() completes before attr teardown — and since A8's state is module-global and never kfree'd, there is NO use-after-free window for A8 regardless of stop ordering (A8's remove-first ordering is therefore a robustness asset only structurally, not load-bearing). (3) None of the three kalloc per-device state, so all three are MORE memory-pressure-robust than A3 itself (which kzallocs nvl->recover).=B — IMPROVED, COEXISTENCE + REVIEW-COST. Judged on: (1) does A8 v2 break A3's existing sysfs attributes; (2) how easily the queued v5 deep review can triangulate A8 v2 against A3; (3) how much NEW surface area each variant adds; (4) does each stay minimally divergent from A3 to amortise reviewer cost. Grounded against the live tree: A3 (nv-tb-egpu-recover.c:786 attr_group, :836 create, :864 remove) and A2 (nv-tb-egpu-qwd.c:362/414/434) both attach flat (.name unset) const attribute_groups to &nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj with disjoint tb_egpu_recover_* / tb_egpu_qwd_* prefixes; A8's proposed names (tb_egpu_state, tb_egpu_f40b_fires, tb_egpu_recovery_count/_failures, tb_egpu_last_recovery_ns) collide with NONE of them. Confirmed the A1 shared TU nv-tb-egpu-pcie.{c,h} EXISTS and is consumed by recover/qwd/close/nv-pci, but contains ZERO sysfs code today (its exports are all tb_egpu_pcie_* PCIe-register reads) — so Variant C's premise is real but it would be the FIRST sysfs code added to that shared TU. Also confirmed A8 v1 (the broken dev_groups version) is NOT in the live tree: no nv_tb_egpu_attr_group / DEVICE_ATTR_RO / dev_groups in nv.c or nv-pci.c (only A6's f40b open-work machinery at nv.c:1825+); task #273 has it pending. So the 'v1->v2 diff churn' all three variants discuss is partly moot — nothing is committed to diff against.=A — CONSERVATIVE (clone A3/A2 idiom into dedicated nv-tb-egpu-metrics.{c,h} TU)
Synthesis chose: Variant A (CONSERVATIVE) base + Variant B's metrics-reset graft; Variant C rejected

a3Pattern.remove_call_context:
A3 remove context (nv-tb-egpu-recover.c:854-884) — runs FIRST in tb_egpu_recover_stop, BEFORE cancel_work_sync, BEFORE pdev_for_work refcount drain, BEFORE kfree(st). The sequence is: NULL guards → sysfs_remove_group → read st = nvl->recover → cancel_work_sync(&st->reset_work) → drain stale refcount → kfree(st) → nvl->recover = NULL.

QWD remove context (nv-tb-egpu-qwd.c:424-455) — runs FIRST in tb_egpu_qwd_stop, BEFORE kthread_stop. The sequence is: NULL guards → sysfs_remove_group → read qwd = nvl->qwd → kthread_stop(qwd->thread) → kfree(qwd) → nvl->qwd = NULL.

Key invariant: sysfs disappears FIRST so userspace can't read counters whose backing state is about to be freed; only THEN does state teardown run.
a3Pattern.parent_kobject_source:
Both A3 and QWD attach to &nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj — the PCI device's struct device embedded kobject. That kobject corresponds to /sys/bus/pci/devices/<DBDF>/ in sysfs (NOT /sys/module/nvidia and NOT a custom kobject).

Provenance of nvl->pci_dev:
- Field declared at common/inc/nv-linux.h:1255 as `struct pci_dev *pci_dev;` inside nv_linux_state_s.
- Assigned exactly once at nv-pci.c:1999 (`nvl->pci_dev = pci_dev;`) inside nv_pci_probe, immediately after the nvl allocation and pci_set_drvdata() — i.e. BEFORE tb_egpu_recover_init runs at nv-pci.c:2008.
- Cleared at nvl free (in remove path); both stop functions guard with `if (nvl->pci_dev)` for defense in depth.

Both functions deliberately omit a NULL check on `&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj` itself — the embedded struct device kobj is initialized by the PCI core well before nv_pci_probe is invoked, so it's guaranteed non-NULL whenever pci_dev itself is non-NULL.
a3Pattern.error_handling_pattern:
A3 (nv-tb-egpu-recover.c:836-841) logs the rc at NV_DBG_INFO (NOT NV_DBG_ERRORS) and otherwise IGNORES the failure. tb_egpu_recover_init still returns 0 — the function explicitly does NOT fail-propagate sysfs_create_group's error. The comment at line 832-833 documents the policy: "Publish sysfs surface. Failure is non-fatal — recovery still runs, just without userspace-visible counters."

QWD (nv-tb-egpu-qwd.c:414-419) is identical in pattern: NV_DBG_INFO log only, no early-return, no state cleanup, return 0 continues. Comment at line 408-411 mirrors A3's: "Publish sysfs surface. Failure is non-fatal — kthread still runs, just without userspace-visible counters."

Downstream impact in nv_pci_probe: `(void)tb_egpu_recover_init(nvl);` at nv-pci.c:2008 and `(void)tb_egpu_qwd_init(nvl);` at nv-pci.c:2305 both cast away the return value entirely — even if the helper DID propagate, the probe path would ignore it. The probe never fails on sysfs-publish failure for either addon.

This matches the architectural intent: sysfs counters are observability/test surface, NOT a load-bearing control path. Losing them must not break the driver.
a3Pattern.lifecycle_ordering:
A3 lifecycle ordering (relative to nv_pci_probe at nv-pci.c:1808):
- tb_egpu_recover_init called at nv-pci.c:2008, very EARLY in probe — AFTER pci_set_drvdata + nvl->pci_dev assignment (lines 1993-1999), BEFORE nv_check_pci_config / IRQ setup / BAR mapping / rm_init_adapter.
- Comment at nv-pci.c:2003-2007 says "allocate per-pdev recovery state early so the probe-time WPR2 check below has nvl->recover available".
- This is BEFORE the device is fully bound (pci_set_drvdata is done but the rest of init is pending) and CRUCIALLY BEFORE rm_init_adapter — which is the whole point: A3 must be armed to catch a post-rmInit-FAIL.

A3 stop at nv_pci_remove_helper (nv-pci.c:2351): tb_egpu_recover_stop called at nv-pci.c:2394, AFTER tb_egpu_qwd_stop (2387) but BEFORE the rest of remove teardown (rm_cleanup_dynamic_power_management, etc.). Comment at 2389-2393: "drain any pending recovery work and free per-pdev state before nvl is torn down".

QWD lifecycle ordering:
- tb_egpu_qwd_init called at nv-pci.c:2305 — VERY LATE in probe, immediately after rm_enable_dynamic_power_management (line 2294) which is the documented "last step" of probe (comment at 2288-2293). This is AFTER rm_init_adapter, AFTER rm_notify_gpu_addition, AFTER nvidia_modeset_probe. By this point the device is fully bound and operational; QWD just needs to start its heartbeat on a known-good device.
- tb_egpu_qwd_stop called at nv-pci.c:2387 — FIRST teardown action in nv_pci_remove_helper, before tb_egpu_recover_stop and before any RM teardown. Comment at 2379-2386 explains why first: "The kthread reads nv->regs->map and calls os_pci_set_disconnected(nv->handle); both must remain valid until the kthread has exited".

Symmetry rule: A3 first-in / last-out (init early in probe / stop late but before RM cleanup), QWD last-in / first-out (init at end of probe / stop at start of remove). Both stop calls run before the rest of remove tears down nvl/nv state.
gotchas: sysfs_create_group on &nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj attaches files directly to /sys/bus/pci/devices/<DBDF>/ — NOT to /sys/module/nvidia/. The driver registers no custom kobject. The same path is used by uevent emission (nv-tb-egpu-recover.c:183: `kobject_uevent_env(&pdev->dev.kobj, ...)`).

Per-feature kill-switch (NVreg_TbEgpuRecoverEnable=0 / NVreg_TbEgpuQwdEnable=0) causes the init function to return 0 EARLY, before any sysfs_create_group call — so a disabled feature produces no sysfs surface at all, and the matching stop function's unconditional sysfs_remove_group is a benign no-op on the never-created group. A8 v2 must decide whether to mirror this (enable-gated publish) or always publish.

Both addons use `(void)tb_egpu_*_init(nvl);` at the probe call site — return value explicitly discarded. The init helpers themselves return success even when sysfs creation fails. This means the failure mode A8 v1 exhibited (silently absent sysfs files) is EXACTLY the failure mode this pattern degrades to gracefully; the dev_groups mechanism appears to silently no-op without even an NV_DBG_INFO log line on the create path. The sysfs_create_group return value gives you a logged errno to debug from.

Stop functions check `if (nvl->pci_dev)` before sysfs_remove_group even though the same nvl->pci_dev was guaranteed non-NULL at init time. Belt-and-suspenders pattern: cheap NULL guard against an nvl that lost its pci_dev association during a weird teardown path. A8 v2 should mirror this guard.

No locks are taken around sysfs_create_group / sysfs_remove_group in either addon — the PCI probe / remove serialization (driver core holds device_lock for the duration of probe/remove callbacks) provides exclusion against concurrent state changes. A8 v2 inheriting the same call sites in nv_pci_probe_helper / nv_pci_remove_helper gets this for free.

QWD's stop ordering is documented load-bearing: sysfs surface removed BEFORE kthread_stop because reader sysfs handlers (e.g. tb_egpu_qwd_last_aer_summary_show at qwd.c:315) deref nvl->qwd which is freed at the end of stop. Removing sysfs first guarantees no concurrent reader can race against the kfree. A8 v2's stop function must use the same order: sysfs_remove_group FIRST, then state teardown / kfree.

DEVICE_ATTR + struct attribute *[] + const struct attribute_group is the canonical kernel 5.x+ pattern; it correctly handles the .mode field per-attribute (no dev_groups-style is_visible callback needed for static permissions like 0444/0200). A8 v1's `pci_driver.driver.dev_groups` path requires the kernel to walk the dev_groups list during driver core's `driver_register` → `device_add` path, and silently no-ops in certain bind scenarios — particularly when the device's bus_type already had a sysfs group registered or when binding happens via a route that bypasses the dev_groups walk (e.g. manual sysfs bind/unbind). Direct sysfs_create_group from probe sidesteps that entire class of failure.

Both addons attach the group with no .name on attribute_group — files land directly in the pdev's sysfs dir. If A8 v2 wants a sub-namespace like /sys/.../<DBDF>/a8/, set .name = "a8" on its attribute_group and the kernel will create the subdir. Without it, A8 files mix with the pdev's existing standard attrs (vendor, device, class, ...) and with A2/A3's `tb_egpu_*` files — which is exactly how A2 and A3 currently arrange themselves, sharing the same flat dir.
a3Pattern.recommendation_for_a8_v2:
Mirror the A3/QWD pattern exactly. Concrete plan:

**File placement.** Put the implementation in `kernel-open/nvidia/nv.c` if A8's globals already live there (per your prompt), OR create a dedicated `kernel-open/nvidia/nv-a8-<name>.c` / `.h` pair mirroring A3's file layout — the latter is cleaner and matches addon-recarve discipline (one addon per TU, no cross-addon source-side reach). Both are acceptable; the dedicated-TU choice is the one A3 and A2 use and is the path of least review friction.

**Attribute group shape.** Define static DEVICE_ATTR entries (one per A8 attribute), a static `struct attribute *a8_attrs[] = { ..., NULL }` array, and a static `const struct attribute_group a8_attr_group = { .attrs = a8_attrs };`. Use 0444 for read-only counters and 0200 for write-only triggers, matching A3. If you want A8's files namespaced under a subdir, add `.name = "a8"` to the attribute_group; otherwise leave it unset to land alongside `tb_egpu_recover_*` and `tb_egpu_qwd_*` in the flat pdev dir.

**Init function signature.** `int a8_init(nv_linux_state_t *nvl)` returning 0. Steps inside, in order: enable-flag short-circuit (return 0 leaving nvl->a8 NULL if disabled); kzalloc state; init counters; publish `nvl->a8 = state;` BEFORE the sysfs call so any concurrent sysfs reader sees consistent state; THEN `if (nvl->pci_dev) { int rc = sysfs_create_group(&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj, &a8_attr_group); if (rc != 0) NV_DEV_PRINTF(NV_DBG_INFO, nv, "a8: sysfs_create_group failed: %d\n", rc); }`; return 0 unconditionally (the existing `(void)` cast at the call site means propagating a failure would be ignored anyway).

**Stop function signature.** `void a8_stop(nv_linux_state_t *nvl)`. Steps: NULL-guard `if (!nvl) return;`; `if (nvl->pci_dev) sysfs_remove_group(&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj, &a8_attr_group);` FIRST so concurrent readers can't race against kfree; THEN tear down state; THEN `kfree(nvl->a8); nvl->a8 = NULL;`.

**Call sites in nv-pci.c.**
- In `nv_pci_probe` (currently nv-pci.c:1808): insert `(void)a8_init(nvl);` — preferred placement late in probe alongside the QWD call at line 2305 (after `rm_enable_dynamic_power_management`) IF A8 needs the device fully bound; or early next to the A3 call at line 2008 IF A8 must be armed before rm_init_adapter. Default to the QWD location unless A8 has a specific pre-rmInit dependency.
- In `nv_pci_remove_helper` (nv-pci.c:2351): insert `a8_stop(nvl);` immediately before or after the existing `tb_egpu_qwd_stop(nvl);` at line 2387 — symmetric with the chosen init position. Stop calls run early in remove before any RM teardown.

**What this fixes vs A8 v1.** `pci_driver.driver.dev_groups` is registered once at driver_register time and the driver core is supposed to apply it during `device_add` for each bound device. The mechanism can silently no-op if the dev_groups field isn't yet set at the right moment in driver-core init, or if the binding takes a path that bypasses the per-device sysfs population (manual bind via /sys/bus/pci/drivers/.../bind, hotplug rebind after surprise-removal, etc.). Direct `sysfs_create_group` from inside the probe callback is unconditional and well-tested in this codebase (A2 and A3 both ship with it and the surface is consistently present in /sys/bus/pci/devices/<DBDF>/). It also gives you a logged errno on failure instead of the silent absence you're seeing with v1.

**Naming guidance.** Mirror A3's prefix discipline — symbol names start with `a8_` (or whatever the addon's canonical short name is) so a future addon-recarve audit can grep for cross-addon reach. Per-attr names like `dev_attr_a8_<metric>` keep the flat pdev sysfs dir self-documenting.

**Verification path post-change.** After the rebuild, `ls /sys/bus/pci/devices/<DBDF>/ | grep ^a8_` should show the A8 files alongside `tb_egpu_recover_*` and `tb_egpu_qwd_*`. If they're absent, check `dmesg | grep "a8.*sysfs_create_group"` for the NV_DBG_INFO failure line — which is itself a strict improvement over v1's silent failure mode.

a8Failure.a8_v1_mechanism_used: A8 v1 sets `.driver.dev_groups = nv_tb_egpu_dev_groups` (the INNER `struct device_driver`'s field, nested inside `struct pci_driver`), at /root/nvidia-driver-injector/patches/addon/A8-f40b-sysfs-observability.patch line ~59 (post-application line ~3057 of nv-pci.c). It relies on the driver core's "automatic" attribute-group attach-on-bind machinery, which reads `device_driver.dev_groups` in drivers/base/dd.c at the `device_add_groups(dev, drv->dev_groups)` call site.
a8Failure.failure_evidence: Symbols ARE loaded — /proc/kallsyms (verified live on the running aorus.19 kernel) shows: `dev_attr_tb_egpu_state`, `dev_attr_tb_egpu_f40b_fires`, `dev_attr_tb_egpu_recovery_count`, `dev_attr_tb_egpu_recovery_failures`, `dev_attr_tb_egpu_last_recovery_ns`, `nv_tb_egpu_attrs`, `nv_tb_egpu_attr_group`, `nv_tb_egpu_dev_groups`, `nv_pci_driver` — all present at addresses 0xffffffffc2e49xxx / 0xffffffffc2804xxx (all `[nvidia]`). But sysfs attach failed: `ls /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:04:00.0/` shows NO `tb_egpu_state`/`tb_egpu_f40b_fires`/`tb_egpu_recovery_*`/`tb_egpu_last_recovery_ns` entries. `ls /sys/bus/pci/drivers/nvidia/0000:04:00.0/` shows ONLY A2's `tb_egpu_qwd_*` (5 attrs) and A3's `tb_egpu_recover_*` (5 attrs) — both of which use explicit `sysfs_create_group(&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj, ...)` at probe time (A2-bus-loss-watchdog.patch:491 and A3-recovery.patch:1135). The device IS bound to the nvidia driver (`/sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:04:00.0/driver` → `../../../../../../bus/pci/drivers/nvidia`), so bind-time hooks would have fired if wired correctly.
a8Failure.kernel_dev_groups_expected_behaviour: Per /lib/modules/7.0.9-204.fc44.x86_64/build/include/linux/device/driver.h:79-80 and :118, `struct device_driver.dev_groups` is "Additional attributes attached to device instance once it is bound to the driver". The wiring is in /root/linux-v6.19/drivers/base/dd.c:680: `ret = device_add_groups(dev, drv->dev_groups);` inside `really_probe()` — immediately after successful `->probe()` return. The matching teardown is at dd.c:568: `device_remove_groups(dev, dev->driver->dev_groups);` in the unbind path. So a non-NULL `device_driver.dev_groups` at the moment `really_probe()` runs would create the attrs under `/sys/bus/pci/devices/<bdf>/` (the dev_groups path) AND, via symlink, under `/sys/bus/pci/drivers/<name>/<bdf>/`.

hypotheses.hypothesis: PCI subsystem CLOBBERS device_driver.dev_groups during pci_register_driver. The struct pci_driver has its OWN `dev_groups` field (linux/pci.h:1032, outer-level alongside `.driver`), and __pci_register_driver in drivers/pci/pci-driver.c:1442 unconditionally executes `drv->driver.dev_groups = drv->dev_groups;` — overwriting whatever the patch put into the inner field with the outer field's value. Since A8 v1 only set `.driver.dev_groups` (inner) and NOT `.dev_groups` (outer), the outer is NULL, so the kernel writes NULL into the inner field before driver_register() is called, before any probe runs, before really_probe() ever reads drv->dev_groups for device_add_groups. Net effect: device_add_groups(dev, NULL) is a no-op, no attrs created, no error logged.
hypotheses.supporting_evidence: EXACT line: /root/linux-v6.19/drivers/pci/pci-driver.c:1442 `drv->driver.dev_groups = drv->dev_groups;` inside __pci_register_driver(). The pci_driver struct definition at /lib/modules/7.0.9-204.fc44.x86_64/build/include/linux/pci.h:1019-1036 shows two separate fields: line 1032 `const struct attribute_group **dev_groups;` (PCI-level, outer) and line 1033 `struct device_driver driver;` (which contains its OWN dev_groups at driver.h:118). The patch's `.driver.dev_groups = ...` C99 designated initializer syntax targets the INNER field (correct C, but wrong field for PCI drivers). nv_pci_register_driver() at nv-pci.c:3075 calls pci_register_driver(&nv_pci_driver), so this code path absolutely runs. Existing field `.driver.pm = &nv_pm_ops` and `.driver.probe_type = PROBE_FORCE_SYNCHRONOUS` are also inner-level — but those fields are NOT clobbered by __pci_register_driver (only name, bus, owner, mod_name, groups, dev_groups get assigned), explaining why .driver.pm works but .driver.dev_groups silently doesn't.
hypotheses.refuting_evidence: None found. The smoking-gun line is unambiguous; the patch's clobber mechanism perfectly matches the observed symptom (symbols present + sysfs empty + no error in dmesg, because device_add_groups(dev, NULL) is a silent no-op).

hypotheses.hypothesis: NVIDIA driver registers nv_pci_driver via a non-standard path that bypasses the dev_groups attach. E.g. a custom probe wrapper that does NOT call device_add_groups, or an alternate driver-registration helper.
hypotheses.supporting_evidence: NVIDIA driver does have project-specific stub layering (nv-pci.c comment line 2881 mentions 'nv_pci_driver were registered by the base layer (C4) with stub'). NVIDIA driver also does some odd things historically.
hypotheses.refuting_evidence: nv_pci_register_driver() at nv-pci.c:3069-3076 is a straightforward `return pci_register_driver(&nv_pci_driver);` — no wrapper, no custom path. C4 stub layering refers to err_handler scaffold, not registration mechanism. dd.c:680 device_add_groups runs from really_probe() which is the universal driver-core path; no PCI driver can bypass it without reimplementing driver_register. If A2/A3's explicit sysfs_create_group calls work at probe time, then probe DEFINITELY runs through the standard driver-core path.

hypotheses.hypothesis: C99 designated-initializer .driver.X nested syntax is being miscompiled or NV_API_CALL / similar calling-convention macros corrupt the struct layout, so the dev_groups field is written to the wrong offset.
hypotheses.supporting_evidence: NVIDIA driver uses some unusual macros (NV_API_CALL, NV_WEAK, NV_STATUS). Nested designators .driver.pm and .driver.probe_type are older / more battle-tested than .driver.dev_groups (dev_groups was added in 2014, but field nesting was unusual at the time).
hypotheses.refuting_evidence: C99 nested designated initializers (`.driver.dev_groups = X` inside a struct-of-struct) are bog-standard C99 and have been since GCC 2.95. Other patches in this file already use `.driver.pm` and `.driver.probe_type` and those work (pm callbacks fire, probe_type is honored). NV_API_CALL is a function-attribute macro (for stdcall on Windows / no-op on Linux), not a struct-layout modifier. No symbol-table evidence of corruption — nv_tb_egpu_dev_groups is at a clean rodata address (0xffffffffc2e49150) and the array NULL-terminator pattern is correct (otherwise the kernel would have crashed walking it).

hypotheses.hypothesis: device_add_groups failed silently because the device was already past the bind point when the driver loaded (race: device already enumerated, then driver registered, somehow really_probe ran without dev_groups visible).
hypotheses.supporting_evidence: GPU is hotplugged via Thunderbolt — bind ordering can be unusual; entrypoint sequencing (modprobe → settle → nvidia-smi -pm 1) creates potential for races.
hypotheses.refuting_evidence: device_add_groups runs INSIDE really_probe() which runs INSIDE the driver_register → bus iteration on EVERY bind, including late-binding hotplug events. dd.c:680-684 also explicitly dev_err()'s on failure — kernel ring buffer would show 'device_add_groups() failed' if it had been called with valid groups and failed. The clobber hypothesis explains the SILENCE: device_add_groups(dev, NULL) is documented no-op.

a8Failure.best_root_cause_explanation: A8 v1's `.driver.dev_groups = nv_tb_egpu_dev_groups` assignment is silently nullified by the PCI subsystem during `pci_register_driver()`. The mechanism is precise and unambiguous: `struct pci_driver` contains TWO distinct fields named `dev_groups` — an outer one at the PCI level (linux/pci.h:1032) and an inner one inside the embedded `struct device_driver driver` (linux/device/driver.h:118). The driver core reads ONLY the inner field (`device_driver.dev_groups`) when attaching attributes at bind time, in drivers/base/dd.c:680: `device_add_groups(dev, drv->dev_groups)` (where drv here is struct device_driver*). But `__pci_register_driver()` in drivers/pci/pci-driver.c:1442 unconditionally executes `drv->driver.dev_groups = drv->dev_groups;` — copying the OUTER field (PCI-level) INTO the inner field, OVERWRITING anything the patch placed there. Because A8 v1 set only the inner field and left the outer one at NULL (the zero-init default), `__pci_register_driver` overwrites the patched non-NULL inner pointer with NULL before `driver_register()` is even called. At bind time, `device_add_groups(dev, NULL)` is a silent no-op (no error path, no dmesg line), perfectly matching the observed symptom: symbols loaded fine (group structures exist in rodata), nv_pci_driver registration succeeded (probe ran, A2/A3 explicit sysfs_create_group calls succeeded), but the kernel never attached A8's group to the device. The .driver.pm and .driver.probe_type sibling fields are NOT clobbered by __pci_register_driver and that is why they work — only `name`, `bus`, `owner`, `mod_name`, `groups`, and `dev_groups` get assigned from the outer struct. The fix is a one-character change: `.dev_groups = nv_tb_egpu_dev_groups` (outer field, the documented PCI API), not `.driver.dev_groups = ...`. However, given the existing A2 and A3 patches ALREADY use explicit `sysfs_create_group(&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj, ...)` at probe time (A2-bus-loss-watchdog.patch:491, A3-recovery.patch:1135) for their tb_egpu_qwd_* and tb_egpu_recover_* attributes — and those are proven working — the strongest recommendation is consistency: A8 v2 should follow the same pattern.
a8Failure.why_a8_v2_should_use_sysfs_create_group_: Three reasons, in priority order. (1) Architectural consistency with the existing addon-layer convention: A2 (tb_egpu_qwd_attr_group, A2-bus-loss-watchdog.patch:491) and A3 (tb_egpu_recover_attr_group, A3-recovery.patch:1135) both use explicit `sysfs_create_group(&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj, &<group>)` at their respective init points and `sysfs_remove_group` at stop points. These are LIVE AND WORKING — the 10 tb_egpu_qwd_* + tb_egpu_recover_* attrs are visible in /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:04:00.0/ right now. A8 using a different mechanism creates two implementation patterns to maintain. (2) Avoids the pci_driver dev_groups footgun entirely: the outer-vs-inner field aliasing in struct pci_driver is exactly the kind of subtle kernel API mistake that has wasted a deployment cycle here (image apnex/nvidia-driver-injector:595.71.05-aorus.19) and could recur on future maintenance touches. Even with the correct `.dev_groups = ...` syntax, future patch refactors could re-introduce the bug. The explicit sysfs_create_group call is unambiguous and self-documenting. (3) Lifetime control: explicit probe-time create lets A8 fail-soft with a debug print (the A2 pattern: `NV_DEV_PRINTF(NV_DBG_INFO, ...)` on rc != 0) instead of the dev_groups path's silent failure mode where a NULL group pointer produces no error at all. For an observability surface — which is explicitly diagnostic infrastructure — silent failure is worst-case behavior. Recommended A8 v2: add a tb_egpu_metrics_init(nvl) call near A2's tb_egpu_qwd_start invocation in nv_pci_probe (post-bind, with nvl->pci_dev valid) that does `sysfs_create_group(&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj, &nv_tb_egpu_attr_group)` with the same fail-soft pattern, and tb_egpu_metrics_stop(nvl) symmetrically before A2's tb_egpu_qwd_stop in nv_pci_remove. The 5 attrs, group definition, and atomic state struct from v1 carry forward unchanged; only the registration mechanism in nv-pci.c flips from a struct-field assignment to a probe-path function call.

sysfsAlts.recommendation: Use `sysfs_create_group(&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj, &nv_tb_egpu_attr_group)` in nvidia.ko's PCI `probe` path and `sysfs_remove_group(...)` in the `remove` path — exactly matching the A3-recovery pattern already shipping on this driver. Rationale: (1) A8 v1's `dev_groups` mechanism is the thing being replaced (task title: "replace failing dev_groups mechanism"), so we need an explicit, code-driven registration call; (2) A3 has empirically proven `sysfs_create_group` works on `&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj` at nv_linux_state_t init/stop — same kobj, same lifetime hooks, same nvl access pattern; (3) the v1 → v2 diff collapses to roughly four lines (one create + one remove + drop the `dev_groups` line + drop the extern in nv-pci.c); (4) reviewers already approved this idiom for A3 last cycle, so review cost is near zero; (5) the existing 5-attr `attribute_group` struct from v1 is reused verbatim — only the registration site changes. Avoid `devm_device_add_group` (devres release timing is tied to `struct device` teardown, which on PCI happens after the driver's `remove` callback returns — fine in theory, but nvidia.ko already does explicit cleanup in `remove` and mixing devres with manual teardown is the kind of asymmetry that produces leak-on-unbind regressions). Avoid `device_create_file` (5 calls, 5 error paths, 5 reverse calls — A3's own refactor commit log explicitly cites consolidating away from this pattern as a win, per legacy/0003-tb-egpu-qwatchdog.patch line 35). Avoid `device_type.groups` (requires owning the bus's `device_type`, which nvidia.ko does not — PCI core owns it). `sysfs_create_files` is workable but loses the named-group container; `sysfs_create_group` is strictly better since the v1 attribute_group is already written.

alternatives.signature: int sysfs_create_group(struct kobject *kobj, const struct attribute_group *grp); void sysfs_remove_group(struct kobject *kobj, const struct attribute_group *grp);
pros: Already proven on this exact driver: A3-recovery.patch calls it on &nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj at tb_egpu_recover_init and removes it at tb_egpu_recover_stop
Reuses A8 v1's attribute_group struct verbatim — only the registration site moves from .driver.dev_groups to a code call
Explicit lifecycle: registration site and removal site are both visible in the patch, easy to audit
Reviewers already accepted this idiom for A3 in a prior cycle — amortises review cost
Minimal v1→v2 diff (~4 lines: 1 create, 1 remove, drop extern, drop dev_groups field assignment)
Non-fatal-on-failure pattern already established in A3 (warn-and-continue), directly copyable
cons: Driver code must call both create and remove — error path discipline required (but trivial for a single group)
Slightly less 'declarative' than dev_groups (but that's the v1 mechanism we're moving away from)
alternatives.rationale: Best fit on three independent axes: empirical proof on this codebase (A3 ships it), structural fit (nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj is the same kobj A3 uses, same lifetime), and minimum-divergence-from-A3 to amortise review. The attribute_group already exists in A8 v1; only the registration call site changes.

alternatives.signature: int sysfs_create_files(struct kobject *kobj, const struct attribute * const *ptr); void sysfs_remove_files(struct kobject *kobj, const struct attribute * const *ptr);
pros: Conceptually simpler if there is no named subdirectory — just a flat array of attributes attached directly to the kobj
Same kobj target as sysfs_create_group, so lifetime story is identical
Single call create / single call remove
cons: Loses the attribute_group container that A8 v1 already wrote — would need to convert the struct attribute_group back to a NULL-terminated struct attribute * array
Inconsistent with A3's already-merged pattern — divergence increases review burden
No advantage over sysfs_create_group when the attribute set is already grouped
If a named subdirectory is ever wanted (.name in attribute_group), can't add it without migrating
alternatives.rationale: Functionally equivalent for 5 flat attributes, but strictly worse for A8 because v1's code already defines an attribute_group. Choosing this means rewriting the group declaration AND diverging from A3 — both are pure cost with no benefit.

alternatives.signature: int device_create_file(struct device *dev, const struct device_attribute *attr); void device_remove_file(struct device *dev, const struct device_attribute *attr);
pros: Takes struct device * directly (no kobj indirection)
One-attribute-at-a-time granularity if attributes are conditional
cons: 5 attributes = 5 create calls + 5 reverse remove calls + 5 error-path unwind branches

Legacy patches 0015/0016/0023/0026 used exactly this idiom and were explicitly consolidated AWAY from it during the P3/0003 refactor (legacy/0003-tb-egpu-qwatchdog.patch:35 documents the win: '5 separate device_create_file / device_remove_file → sysfs_create_group / sysfs_remove_group')
Re-introducing this idiom regresses the project's own refactor history
Diverges from A3 — opposite of the 'minimum divergence' goal
alternatives.rationale: Explicitly avoid: the project has a documented refactor that moved away from this pattern. Reintroducing it would invite the same code-review pushback that prompted the original consolidation.

alternatives.signature: int devm_device_add_group(struct device *dev, const struct attribute_group *grp);
pros: Auto-removed when the struct device is released — no explicit remove call needed
Eliminates one class of leak (forget to remove on probe-fail or remove path)
Single call site
cons: Devres release fires when the struct device's devres list is dropped, which on PCI is tied to device release, not to driver remove — semantics differ subtly from explicit sysfs_remove_group at unbind

nvidia.ko's existing teardown is explicit and synchronous (A3 calls sysfs_remove_group in tb_egpu_recover_stop); mixing devres-managed and manually-managed sysfs on the same dev produces asymmetric lifetimes that are a known source of unbind-time bugs
Diverges from A3's pattern — fails the 'minimum divergence to amortise review' criterion
If A9 later wants to drive removal earlier than device release (e.g. on a recovery surrender path), devm_* removes that option

Combined with E1 classification: attributes attach to every bound device, but the devres list belongs to the underlying struct device — re-bind/re-probe interactions need extra thought that explicit create/remove avoids
alternatives.rationale: Workable in isolation, but the lifetime semantics don't exactly match how nvidia.ko already manages teardown (synchronous, explicit, in remove callback). Adopting devres for ONE attribute group while everything around it is manual creates an asymmetry that is hard to reason about. Avoid in favour of the proven A3 pattern.

alternatives.signature: struct device_driver { ... const struct attribute_group **dev_groups; ... };  /* set via pci_driver.driver.dev_groups */
pros: Fully declarative — kernel handles create/remove automatically at bind/unbind
Zero driver-code involvement in attribute lifecycle
Idiomatic for new code in modern kernels
cons: This is the mechanism A8 v1 used and is the thing being replaced for v2 (task title: 'replace failing dev_groups mechanism')
Whatever broke v1 (likely an ordering / classification / per-device storage issue, or attributes appearing on non-E1 devices) is structural to this approach
No control over WHICH bound devices get the attributes — kernel attaches them to every PCI device nvidia.ko binds, which conflicts with 'E1-classified eGPU only' if A8 v2 wants gating
Cannot attach extra per-device state initialization to the same site — registration is implicit
alternatives.rationale: Explicitly out of scope for v2 — this is the failing mechanism. The task is to pick its replacement. Listed here for completeness so the rationale of moving away is documented.

pros: Declarative; per-device-type granularity
Used by bus subsystems that own their device_type (e.g. tty, input)
cons: PCI core owns the device_type for pci_dev — nvidia.ko cannot override it without forking the PCI core, which is far outside scope
Architectural mismatch: device_type is for the type that creates the device, not for the driver that binds to it
Diverges from every existing pattern in nvidia.ko
alternatives.rationale: Architecturally inapplicable: nvidia.ko is a driver that binds to a PCI device, not the bus that registers the device. The PCI core owns device_type for struct pci_dev; nvidia.ko has no hook to set it.

alternatives.signature: int sysfs_add_file_to_group(struct kobject *kobj, const struct attribute *attr, const char *group); void sysfs_remove_file_from_group(struct kobject *kobj, const struct attribute *attr, const char *group);
pros: Allows adding a single attribute into an existing named group at runtime
Useful when attribute availability is conditional on runtime feature detection
cons: A8's 5 attributes are static and unconditional — no need for runtime add/remove granularity
Requires the group to already exist; doesn't solve the registration problem on its own
More API surface than the use case warrants
alternatives.rationale: Mismatched granularity. A8's attribute set is fixed at build time; the runtime add/remove machinery this API provides is solving a different problem.

testBPlan.test_question: Primary (candidate a, sharpened): "When rmmod reaches nv_shutdown_adapter with NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED still set (persistence engaged) on a chip whose C5 sink was ALREADY set by a prior A6 open-path fire (cycle-2 open on the F40-precondition substrate), does rm_disable_adapter / rm_shutdown_adapter FAST-FAIL on the pre-existing C5 sink (A7 wrapper logs 'completed within budget' for both, matching the patch intent's original prediction) — OR does rm_shutdown_adapter STILL time out structurally at 200 ms exactly like Test A despite the sink being set?" Secondary (candidate b): "Does persistence-engaged rmmod traverse A7's wrap at all, and does it hit A7's timeout branch differently from the non-persistence Test B path (which skipped nv_shutdown_adapter entirely via the nv_pci_remove_helper NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED short-circuit)?" The two collapse into one run: persistence keeps NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED set, which is precisely what forces rmmod down the nv_shutdown_adapter path Test B never exercised, so A7's wrap is reached and we can finally read whether the C5 sink fast-fails the RM calls or whether the structural 200 ms hang is sink-independent.
testBPlan.delta_vs_test_b: Insert one step — `nvidia-smi -pm 1` (persistence engage) — into Phase 1 AFTER `modprobe --ignore-install nvidia` and BEFORE the Phase 2 cycle-1 open, so NV_FLAG_PERSISTENT_SW_STATE keeps NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED set across LAST-CLOSE and the Phase 3 rmmod consequently calls nv_shutdown_adapter (reaching A7's wrap) instead of short-circuiting through nv_pci_remove_helper as Test B did; everything else in the F40-precondition recipe + cycle-1/cycle-2 + rmmod sequence is identical to Test B.

expected_outcomes.variant: Variant A — RM fast-fails on the pre-set C5 sink (patch-intent's original prediction confirmed)
expected_outcomes.mechanism: A6 fired on the Phase 2 cycle-2 open and set the C5 sink (PDB_PROP_GPU_IS_LOST) via rm_cleanup_gpu_lost_state. Persistence kept NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED set, so Phase 3 rmmod's nv_pci_remove_helper calls nv_shutdown_adapter, which calls rm_disable_adapter then rm_shutdown_adapter. RM closed code checks the sink at a sink-aware gate, observes GPU-already-lost, and returns immediately WITHOUT issuing the chip-touching MMIO that hangs. A7's wrapper sees both calls return inside the 200 ms budget.
expected_outcomes.observability: journalctl -k shows: 'rm_disable_adapter scheduled' + 'rm_disable_adapter completed within budget' THEN 'rm_shutdown_adapter scheduled' + 'rm_shutdown_adapter completed within budget' (TWO 'completed within budget' lines, NO 'timed out' line), followed by 'nvidia-nvlink: Unregistered Nvlink Core'. rmmod returns 0 in well under 1 s. Distinct from both Test A (which had a timeout line) and Test B (which had ZERO F40b lines).

expected_outcomes.variant: Variant B — RM still hangs structurally; A7 times out on rm_shutdown_adapter just like Test A (sink-independent structural hang)
expected_outcomes.mechanism: Either A6 did NOT fire/set the sink on the cycle-2 open (chip stayed responsive that cycle), or the C5 sink does NOT gate the specific chip-touching MMIO inside rm_shutdown_adapter. rmmod calls nv_shutdown_adapter; rm_disable_adapter completes within budget (chip responsive to that call, as in Test A); rm_shutdown_adapter issues its MMIO, the chip withholds completion, and A7's bounded worker times out at 200 ms, sets the C5 sink, and returns. nv_shutdown_adapter finishes host-side teardown; rmmod returns 0. This would prove the rm_shutdown_adapter hang is structural and sink-independent — refuting the patch intent's 'RM observes sink and fast-fails' prediction even when the sink is demonstrably pre-set.
expected_outcomes.observability: journalctl -k shows the byte-identical Test A signature: 'rm_disable_adapter completed within budget' THEN 'rm_shutdown_adapter timed out after 200 ms — declaring GPU lost (detector_class=3 DETECTOR_AER_FATAL); worker leaked', then 'nvidia-nvlink: Unregistered Nvlink Core'. Wall-clock for rmmod ~1 s (one 200 ms timeout). The presence of the 'timed out' line on a chip that A6 already sink-set in Phase 2 is the discriminator.

expected_outcomes.variant: Variant C — chained A6→A7 wedge escapes both wrappers (failure-of-containment, low-probability but must be named)
expected_outcomes.mechanism: On a doubly-stressed substrate (A6 already fired + persistence-engaged teardown), the rm_shutdown_adapter MMIO blocks the CPU and the kernel deadlocks before A7's bounded worker's timeout can be observed (the same AER-vs-deadlock race that wedged 11/12 open-path attempts in F40 Test B v2/VERIFY/TBv2-n2). A7's worker-hop is designed to defeat exactly this by moving the blocking MMIO off the syscall thread, but it has only been validated on singly-stressed (healthy or close-path) chips, never on an A6-pre-fired substrate.
expected_outcomes.observability: journalctl -k goes SILENT after 'rm_shutdown_adapter scheduled to bounded worker' with NO matching 'completed' or 'timed out' line within ~5 s; systemd/k3s timers stop; the rmmod process is stuck in D-state. This is the abort trigger and the reboot-recovery case.

procedure.phase: Phase 0 — pre-flight baseline (NEW vs Test B; mandatory because chip just rebooted)
procedure.action: Capture pre-state PASSIVELY: BAR1 size from sysfs FIRST (never nvidia-smi first on a post-replug chip per no-rpc-observability-on-broken-bar1), then module refcount, uptime, load, and the A7 timeout param. Record T0 for journalctl windowing.
procedure.command: T0=$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S'); echo "T0=$T0"; sed -n '2p' /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:04:00.0/resource; grep -E '^nvidia' /proc/modules; cat /sys/module/nvidia/parameters/NVreg_TbEgpuShutdownTimeoutMs; uptime
procedure.expected: BAR1 second resource line spans 32 GiB (0x...800000000 range = 0x800000000 bytes); nvidia + nvidia_uvm loaded; NVreg_TbEgpuShutdownTimeoutMs=200; host alive. Confirms healthy substrate and A7 active before we touch the chip.
procedure.safety_concern: none — all reads are passive sysfs/procfs. Do NOT run nvidia-smi here; BAR1 must be confirmed 32 GiB by sysfs before any RPC-issuing tool touches the chip.

procedure.action: Graceful host-side teardown of the running injector driver via the pod's uninstall subcommand (this fires A7 once during the healthy unload — sink set or timeout, host-side cleanup completes, modules unload). This is the same Phase 1 step 1 as Test B.
procedure.command: POD=$(kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app=nvidia-driver-injector -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}'); echo "pod=$POD"; kubectl exec -n kube-system "$POD" -- /entrypoint.sh uninstall
procedure.expected: entrypoint prints 'node-label removed / rmmod nvidia_uvm / rmmod nvidia / uninstall ok'; /proc/modules clean afterward. A7 fires once here (expected per Test A — rm_shutdown_adapter timeout on this healthy unload).
procedure.safety_concern: This rmmod itself exercises A7 on a healthy chip; per Test A n=2 this is host-safe, but it IS a chip-touch and the first wedge opportunity. If host goes silent here, abort and reboot.

procedure.action: TB deauthorize then reauthorize to drive the chip into broken-BAR1 (F41), establishing the userspace-recovered substrate F40-open requires.
procedure.expected: Device re-enumerates; BAR1 now reports broken (256 MiB) on sysfs. ~6 s wall-clock total.
procedure.safety_concern: TB reauth is a surprise-removal-adjacent operation; with the driver already unloaded (step 2) there is no consumer holding the device, so the surprise-removal wedge class does not apply here. Safe given module-unloaded ordering.

procedure.action: Clear the memory-decode bit (fix-bar1 safety precondition), restore BAR1 to 32 GiB via fix-bar1.sh, then clear the driver_override fix-bar1 leaves at 'none'.
procedure.expected: fix-bar1.sh writes ReBAR CTRL=0x00000f21, pciehp slot-cycles, BAR1 back to 32 GiB in ~7 s; driver_override now empty. State captures land in /var/log/fix-bar1-<ts>/.
procedure.safety_concern: none beyond the documented fix-bar1 behaviour; this is the proven recovery path from F40 catalog, exercised cleanly in Test B.

procedure.action: Load the patched module manually (host policy has 'install nvidia /bin/false', so --ignore-install is required; NOT via the pod, for control parity with Test B).
procedure.expected: nvidia.ko binds to 0000:04:00.0; /dev/nvidia0 + /dev/nvidiactl present; nvidia-nvlink core init line in journal; 'external GPU detected' (E1) line. BAR1 still 32 GiB.
procedure.safety_concern: modprobe-spin watch begins here: Test B's Phase 4 saw modprobe spin at 99.6% CPU for 7+ min AFTER nvidia.ko was fully loaded. If modprobe does not return within ~30 s, jump to abort/recovery — do not SIGKILL-and-wait (Test B showed SIGKILL did not terminate it).

procedure.phase: Phase 1 — PERSISTENCE ENGAGE (THE DELTA from Test B)
procedure.action: Engage persistence so NV_FLAG_PERSISTENT_SW_STATE is set and NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED stays set across LAST-CLOSE — this is what forces the Phase 3 rmmod down the nv_shutdown_adapter path that reaches A7's wrap. BAR1 was already confirmed 32 GiB at step 4, so this nvidia-smi is safe to run.
procedure.expected: 'Persistence mode is already Enabled' or 'Enabled for GPU 0000:04:00.0'; rc=0. A6 open-path wrap fires here on the persistence-engage open (per Test A side-observation) and completes within budget. Persistence now engaged.
procedure.safety_concern: nvidia-smi issues RPC/MMIO. SAFE here ONLY because BAR1 was passively confirmed 32 GiB at step 4 and the chip is freshly recovered. If step 4's BAR1 had been broken this call could wedge per no-rpc-observability-on-broken-bar1 — re-confirm 32 GiB before running if any doubt.

procedure.phase: Phase 2 — cycle 1 (clean) then cycle 2 (A6 may fire)
procedure.action: Cycle 1: a clean chip-touching open/close. With persistence engaged, LAST-CLOSE does NOT run nv_stop_device teardown (the Test B close-path A7 fire is suppressed by design — that is the point).
procedure.expected: Clean single-GPU listing (e.g. 'GPU 0: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090'); rc=0; <1 s. Crucially NO close-path A7 fire in the journal (persistence prevents nv_stop_device), unlike Test B's cycle-1 which DID fire A7 on LAST-CLOSE.
procedure.safety_concern: RPC/MMIO call; safe on the confirmed-32GiB substrate. The absence of an A7 close-path fire here (vs Test B) is itself a checkpoint that persistence took effect — if A7 fires on this LAST-CLOSE, persistence did NOT engage and the run is invalid (treat as soft-abort, see abort criteria).

procedure.phase: Phase 2 — cycle 2 (A6 fires, sets C5 sink)
procedure.action: Cycle 2: open /dev/nvidia0 directly. On the F40-precondition substrate this hits the F40-open arm; A6's bounded worker times out at 200 ms, sets the C5 sink, returns -EIO. This is what pre-sets the sink that Phase 3 will test rm_shutdown_adapter against.
procedure.expected: bash reports 'Input/output error', rc=1. Journal shows 'open scheduled to bounded worker' then 'open timed out after 200 ms — declaring GPU lost'. C5 sink now set. (If A6 instead completes within budget, the chip stayed responsive and we are in a sink-NOT-set sub-case — note it; rmmod will then test the structural-hang question directly.)
procedure.safety_concern: This is the canonical F40-open wedge trigger. A6 is validated to contain it (n=2 F40B-TEST), but it is a wedge-class operation: if the host goes silent here instead of returning -EIO within ~1 s, the A6 worker-hop lost the AER-vs-deadlock race — abort and reboot.

procedure.phase: Phase 3 — Test B-prime trigger (rmmod WITH NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED set)
procedure.action: rmmod the module. Because persistence kept NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED set, nv_pci_remove_helper now CALLS nv_shutdown_adapter (unlike Test B's short-circuit), reaching A7's rm_disable_adapter + rm_shutdown_adapter wraps on a chip whose sink A6 set in step 8. Run under timeout so a wedge in the rmmod call itself is bounded on the harness side. Start a fresh journal window marker first.
procedure.command: T3=$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S'); echo "T3=$T3"; timeout 20 rmmod nvidia; echo "rmmod rc=$?"
procedure.expected: VARIANT A: two 'completed within budget' lines (disable + shutdown), no timeout, rmmod rc=0 in <1 s. VARIANT B: 'rm_disable_adapter completed within budget' + 'rm_shutdown_adapter timed out after 200 ms', rmmod rc=0 in ~1 s (Test A signature). Either is host-safe and answers the test question. timeout-124 rc = harness-side bound hit = Variant C wedge.
procedure.safety_concern: PRIMARY measurement step and primary wedge opportunity (Variant C). The bash-side `timeout 20` bounds the harness but does NOT bound the kernel — if the kernel deadlocks, rmmod will be stuck in D-state and timeout cannot kill it. A silent journal after 'rm_shutdown_adapter scheduled' is the abort signal.

procedure.action: Capture the F40b journal lines for the Phase 3 window and classify the variant. Passive read only.
procedure.expected: The exact F40b line sequence that discriminates Variant A (two 'completed within budget') from Variant B ('rm_shutdown_adapter timed out after 200 ms') from Test B (zero F40b lines — which MUST NOT occur here, since that would mean nv_shutdown_adapter was skipped and persistence failed to hold NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED).
procedure.safety_concern: none — passive journalctl read.

procedure.action: Confirm modules clean, host alive, BAR1 intact (passive). Then respawn the injector pod ONLY IF the host is healthy. Watch for the Test B Phase 4 modprobe-spin recurrence.
procedure.command: grep -E '^nvidia' /proc/modules || echo 'modules clean'; sed -n '2p' /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:04:00.0/resource; uptime; kubectl delete pod -n kube-system "$POD"
procedure.expected: /proc/modules clean; BAR1 32 GiB; host alive. DaemonSet respawns a new pod; new pod reaches READY in ~50 s with a clean modprobe (NO 99.6% CPU spin). nvidia-smi works after respawn.
procedure.safety_concern: Test B's UNRESOLVED Phase 4 modprobe spin (99.6% CPU, 7+ min, SIGKILL-immune) may recur. If the respawned pod's modprobe spins, do NOT chase it — the test data (steps 9-10) is already captured; elect reboot. Capture PID + /proc/PID/stack + a 5 s perf sample first IF the host is otherwise responsive.

procedure.action: After respawn READY, verify GPU is healthy. BAR1 was confirmed 32 GiB at step 11, so nvidia-smi is safe.
procedure.command: kubectl wait --for=condition=ready pod -n kube-system -l app=nvidia-driver-injector --timeout=120s; nvidia-smi --query-gpu=persistence_mode,temperature.gpu,power.draw,pstate --format=csv,noheader
procedure.expected: Pod READY; nvidia-smi returns 'Enabled, ~36 C, ~22-64 W, P8/P0'. Clean recovery, run complete.
procedure.safety_concern: nvidia-smi safe only after step-11 sysfs BAR1 re-confirmation. If respawn did not reach READY (modprobe spin), skip this and go to reboot recovery.

safety_considerations: Chip just rebooted (22 min uptime confirmed) and is in MAXIMALLY HEALTHY state — full 32 GiB BAR1, persistence-clean, no prior F40 fire this boot. This is the BEST-case substrate; any wedge observed here is a floor, not a worst-case. Do not over-generalize a clean result to chronically-stressed states.

This is a SINGLE chip-touch cycle (n=1). Unlike Test A's n=2 setup there is no soak/reload between repetitions. The result is a single data point; per reliability methodology n>=3 is needed to resolve structural-vs-circumstantial, so frame the outcome as provisional and DO NOT claim 'A7 fast-fails on sink' (or the converse) as validated from this one run.

Test B's Phase 4 modprobe spin (99.6% CPU, R-state, 7+ min, SIGKILL-immune, nvidia-smi 'couldn't communicate') is UNRESOLVED and may recur at step 5 (manual modprobe) or step 11 (pod respawn). Be ready for it: it does NOT invalidate the steps-9/10 measurement, and the correct response is reboot, not chasing the spin. Capture PID/stack/perf only if the host is otherwise responsive.

nvidia-smi (and any RPC/MMIO-issuing observability) WILL WEDGE the host if BAR1 is broken (256 MiB) — per feedback_no_rpc_observability_on_broken_bar1 (cost 2 reboots on 2026-05-28). Therefore EVERY nvidia-smi in this plan (steps 6, 7, 12) is gated on a PRIOR passive sysfs BAR1=32 GiB confirmation (steps 4/11). First check after any chip-state change is ALWAYS the sysfs BAR1 read, never nvidia-smi.

Do NOT chain Test B-prime runs without a reboot between them. The substrate accumulates state across destructive teardowns (F40 'Open questions': cumulative state from multiple destructive teardowns weakens the chip's response capacity — Run 1 survived, Run 2 wedged on the same boot). Each run must start from a fresh reboot to keep the single-variable (persistence engaged) clean.

Step 9 rmmod is the primary Variant-C wedge opportunity: A7's worker-hop has only ever been validated on singly-stressed chips (healthy in Test A, close-path in Test B), NEVER on an A6-pre-fired doubly-stressed substrate. The AER-vs-deadlock race that wedged 11/12 open-path attempts could in principle beat A7's bounded worker here. The bash-side `timeout` bounds the harness but cannot kill a D-state rmmod stuck on blocked MMIO — treat journal-silence-after-'scheduled' as the kernel-deadlock signal.
Step 8 (cycle-2 open) is itself a canonical F40-open wedge trigger; A6 is the only thing containing it. It is validated n=2 but is genuinely a wedge-class operation on this exact substrate.

Passive-observability-first throughout: prefer journalctl -k, sysfs reads, /proc/modules over any active probe; per feedback_observability_perturbs_bug, active observability (bpftrace, CUDA_LAUNCH_BLOCKING) materially changes the failure mode — this plan deliberately uses ONLY the in-driver F40b kernel-log markers (no bpftrace) as Test A/B did.

If persistence fails to engage (step 6 rc!=0, or an A7 close-path fire appears at step 7's LAST-CLOSE), the entire delta-from-Test-B is void — the run would degenerate into a Test B re-run (rmmod short-circuits nv_shutdown_adapter). Detect this at step 7 and soft-abort rather than collect invalid data.

Forensics discipline: set T0/T3 journal markers (steps 1, 9) and preserve the step-10 journal capture + any fix-bar1 state dumps to /var/log/mission-1-archaeology/test-B-prime-<date>/ before any reboot, so an n=1 wedge is not data-lost (mirrors Test B's preservation pattern).
abort_criteria: Kernel journal goes SILENT after 'tb_egpu [F40b]: rm_shutdown_adapter scheduled to bounded worker' (step 9) or after 'open scheduled to bounded worker' (step 8) with NO matching 'completed within budget' / 'timed out after 200 ms' line within ~5 s — this is the Variant-C kernel-deadlock signature; stop, preserve forensics if the host is still partially responsive, reboot.

`timeout 20 rmmod nvidia` (step 9) returns exit code 124 (harness timeout hit) OR the rmmod process is observed in uninterruptible D-state — the rmmod is wedged on blocked MMIO that the bash-side timeout cannot kill; stop and reboot.

Modprobe spin recurs (step 5 or step 11): a modprobe process at ~99.6% CPU in R-state that does not return within ~30 s and is SIGKILL-immune (per Test B Phase 4). Do not chase to root cause; capture PID/stack/perf only if host is responsive, then reboot. Measurement from steps 9-10 is already captured and remains valid.

Persistence did not actually engage: step 6 nvidia-smi -pm 1 returns non-zero, OR an A7 close-path fire ('rm_shutdown_adapter ...') appears in the journal during step 7's nvidia-smi -L LAST-CLOSE. This means NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED would be cleared and the run degenerates to a Test B re-run — soft-abort (the test question cannot be answered) and reboot before retrying.

Any passive sysfs BAR1 read (steps 1, 4, 11) reports a size other than 32 GiB at a point where 32 GiB is expected, AND a subsequent step would issue nvidia-smi/RPC — do NOT run the RPC tool; stop and reboot to avoid the broken-BAR1 wedge.
systemd/k3s timers or other unrelated subsystems stop emitting in journalctl (kernel-wide scheduler wedge symptom from F40), regardless of which step — immediate reboot.
testBPlan.recovery_path_if_wedged: Recovery from ANY wedge is a host reboot — explicitly, per memory feedback_no_rpc_observability_on_broken_bar1 and the F40 catalog's operational guidance, there is NO in-driver recovery for these wedge classes yet (A9 is draft-only). The chip substrate is structurally fine (BAR1 stays 32 GiB through the wedge in all observed cases); only the kernel/userspace is wedged, so a clean reboot fully restores the pre-injector baseline and the DaemonSet re-binds on next boot. Before rebooting, IF the host is still partially responsive: preserve `journalctl -k --since $T0` and any `/var/log/fix-bar1-*` captures into `/var/log/mission-1-archaeology/test-B-prime-<date>/` so the n=1 result survives. Do NOT attempt the userspace rmmod→TB-recycle→fix-bar1→modprobe recovery loop to avoid a reboot — that loop is for clean F40-recovery between healthy runs, not for un-wedging a deadlocked kernel, and Test B already showed SIGKILL on a spun modprobe does not work. Do NOT run nvidia-smi or any RPC tool to 'check on' a suspected-wedged chip — passive reads only, then reboot.

intentAudit.a8_v1_intent_summary: A8 v1 establishes a read-only, per-PCI-device sysfs surface that exposes the F40b-managed state machine and recovery counters (state, F40b fire count, recovery successes/failures, last-recovery timestamp) at /sys/bus/pci/devices/<bdf>/, so monitoring systems (Prometheus, ops dashboards, post-mortem tooling) can track GPU health and F40b fire/recovery outcomes without parsing kernel logs. A8 only establishes the surface and provides the hook functions: A6 (shipped) bumps the fire counter on every bounded-wait timeout, and A9 (forthcoming) will populate the recovery counters and drive state transitions. The patch deliberately adds no recovery behavior, no uevents, no force-trigger, and no per-device storage.
a8_v1_requirements_to_preserve: The attribute location: all five live at /sys/bus/pci/devices/<bdf>/ on every PCI device nvidia.ko binds.

The lifecycle property: attributes MUST be created at device-bind time and removed at device-unbind time (appear on bind, disappear on unbind). This intent is preserved verbatim; only the MECHANISM that delivers it changes in v2.

Scenario 'Sysfs attributes appear on bind' — on bind completion, tb_egpu_state exists and is readable, the four other attributes are present, tb_egpu_state reads 'healthy\n', and each counter reads '0\n'. (The observable contract is preserved; the implementation note about which mechanism guarantees it changes.)

Scenario 'Sysfs attributes disappear on unbind' — after rmmod nvidia or echo > .../driver/unbind, the five attributes are no longer present (the disappearance outcome is preserved even though v2's mechanism differs).

Requirement: A6's F40b timeout path SHALL increment the fires counter AND set state to lost-temporary, with both updates observable via sysfs read by the time A6's bounded wrapper returns -EIO, on every F40b timeout regardless of detector class or whether A9 recovery is enabled.

Requirement: A9 hooks SHALL be provided as exported function symbols today — nv_tb_egpu_f40b_fired(void), nv_tb_egpu_recovery_succeeded(void), nv_tb_egpu_recovery_failed(int permanent) — with their stated semantics, so A8 is decoupled from A9's timeline. Test A confirmed these symbols are already present in kallsyms in v1, so this requirement was already met and should carry forward verbatim.

a8_v1_requirements_to_revise.original_text: The kernel's `pci_driver.driver.dev_groups` mechanism handles both transitions automatically; driver code MUST NOT call `sysfs_create_group` / `sysfs_remove_group` explicitly.
a8_v1_requirements_to_revise.revision: The driver SHALL create the attribute group explicitly via sysfs_create_group() at probe/bind time and remove it via sysfs_remove_group() at unbind/remove time, mirroring A3's working pattern. Driver code MUST manage the attribute-group lifecycle directly; the pci_driver.driver.dev_groups mechanism MUST NOT be relied upon for these attributes.
a8_v1_requirements_to_revise.reason: Empirically falsified. A7 Test A (2026-05-29, report line 163) found A8 v1's dev_groups approach compiles and links (all A8 symbols present in /proc/kallsyms) but produces NO sysfs entries on disk — none of the five attribute paths materialized. A3's pre-existing attributes, registered via explicit sysfs_create_group at probe time, work fine on the same driver. The v1 requirement is now exactly backwards: A8 v2 MUST call sysfs_create_group / sysfs_remove_group. Action item recorded in both Test A (rec #2) and Test B (rec #5, 'boost priority').

a8_v1_requirements_to_revise.original_text: The driver MUST expose, at `/sys/bus/pci/devices/<bdf>/`, the following read-only attributes on every PCI device that nvidia.ko binds (E1-classified or otherwise — the kernel handles add/remove via `pci_driver.driver.dev_groups`):
a8_v1_requirements_to_revise.revision: The driver MUST expose, at /sys/bus/pci/devices/<bdf>/, the following read-only attributes on every PCI device that nvidia.ko binds (E1-classified or otherwise). The driver creates the group at probe-time bind and removes it at unbind, using sysfs_create_group / sysfs_remove_group (A3 pattern).
a8_v1_requirements_to_revise.reason: Same dev_groups parenthetical is repeated in the requirement preamble and is equally false. Removing the dev_groups attribution here keeps the doc internally consistent with the corrected lifecycle requirement; the surface (which devices, which attributes) is unchanged.

a8_v1_requirements_to_revise.original_text: AND   the unbind completed without driver-code involvement in attribute lifecycle
a8_v1_requirements_to_revise.revision: AND   the unbind completed cleanly with the driver's sysfs_remove_group call (or devm-managed teardown) removing the attribute group as part of the remove path
a8_v1_requirements_to_revise.reason: This 'THEN' clause in the 'Sysfs attributes disappear on unbind' scenario asserts the no-driver-involvement property that dev_groups was supposed to provide. With the v2 explicit-call mechanism, the driver IS involved in the attribute lifecycle, so the assertion must be reworded to describe the explicit (or devm-managed) removal while preserving the disappear-on-unbind outcome.

a8_v1_requirements_to_revise.original_text: | Attribute group registered with PCI core (at module init) | (none — kernel handles registration silently via dev_groups) | n/a |
a8_v1_requirements_to_revise.revision: | Attribute group registered on device (at probe/bind) | (none — sysfs_create_group registers silently; no kernel-log line) | n/a |
a8_v1_requirements_to_revise.reason: Telemetry-contract table row references dev_groups and 'at module init'. v2 registers per-device at probe via sysfs_create_group, not via dev_groups at module init. The 'no kernel log emitted' outcome is preserved (A8 still emits nothing), so only the mechanism/timing wording changes — see telemetry_contract_changes.

a8_v1_requirements_to_revise.original_text: - **Vanilla baseline files**: `kernel-open/nvidia/nv.c` (new state + counters + sysfs show functions added after the A6-introduced module parameter), `kernel-open/nvidia/nv-pci.c` (extern declaration + `dev_groups` field added to `nv_pci_driver`).
a8_v1_requirements_to_revise.revision: Vanilla baseline files: kernel-open/nvidia/nv.c (state + counters + sysfs show functions + attribute_group definition + sysfs_create_group/sysfs_remove_group calls wired into the probe/remove paths). The nv-pci.c dev_groups field edit is removed — v2 does not touch nv_pci_driver.driver.dev_groups; the group is attached at probe time on the per-device kobject instead.
a8_v1_requirements_to_revise.reason: The provenance baseline-files list must match the v2 implementation. v1's nv-pci.c dev_groups edit is the part that did not work and is dropped; the create/remove wiring (likely co-located with A3's existing probe-time sysfs work in nv.c) replaces it.

scope_boundaries_still_valid: A8 SHALL NOT drive any recovery action — it exposes state; A9 mutates state through recovery. (Still valid; v2 changes only how attributes are registered, not what A8 does.)

A8 SHALL NOT emit uevents on state transitions — under the in-driver recovery target there is no reactive userspace consumer needing a wakeup; observability wants current state, which sysfs reads provide. (Still valid.)
A8 SHALL NOT add a write-only force_trigger attribute analogous to A3's — F40b's fire is not userspace-test-triggered. (Still valid.)

A8 SHALL NOT implement per-device storage for counters — single-GPU is the deployment target; per-device storage MAY come in a future multi-GPU patch. NOTE: with the v2 explicit sysfs_create_group-per-device pattern, the attribute group attaches to each device's kobject, but the BACKING storage remains global/single-instance; this boundary stays valid, just confirm the v2 wording does not imply per-device counters.

A8 SHALL NOT modify the existing A3 sysfs attributes — A3's bus-reset-cycle counters coexist under their own names without overlap. (Still valid, and v2 strengthens the relationship by reusing A3's registration pattern.)
intentAudit.telemetry_contract_changes: Minimal. A8 continues to emit zero kernel-log messages; state and counters are surfaced exclusively via sysfs, and the telemetry tier stays 'nominal' (A6 and A9 carry the mandatory-tier human-readable log lines). The only contract-table change is cosmetic/mechanism wording in the first row: replace 'Attribute group registered with PCI core (at module init) ... kernel handles registration silently via dev_groups' with 'Attribute group registered on device (at probe/bind) ... sysfs_create_group registers silently, no kernel-log line'. The 'no log emitted' guarantee for registration, F40b-fire counter bumps, and sysfs reads is unchanged, and the note steering log-watchers to A6's 'tb_egpu [F40b]:' markers via journalctl -k -f remains valid. No new events, levels, or formats are introduced.
provenance_updates_needed: Bump the doc to v2: change the front-matter status from 'draft' to reflect the v2 revision, and add an explicit version marker (e.g. v2, 2026-05-30) consistent with how A7's intent tracks v1.1/v1.2 revisions.

Add a 'why v2' / revision-history note recording the A8 v1 dev_groups failure: A8 v1 used pci_driver.driver.dev_groups, which compiled and linked (all symbols in /proc/kallsyms) but produced no sysfs entries on disk on this driver; v2 switches to explicit sysfs_create_group / sysfs_remove_group at probe/remove time, mirroring A3's working pattern.

Cite A7 Test A validation report (docs/missions/mission-1-egpu-hot-plug-hot-power/design/A7-test-A-validation-2026-05-29.md) as the source that empirically discovered the dev_groups no-show (report line 163) and recommended the A3 sysfs_create_group migration (rec #2, line 170). Test A also confirmed A8's three exported hook symbols are present in kallsyms — i.e., the symbol-linkability requirement already passed in v1.

Cite A7 Test B validation report (docs/missions/mission-1-egpu-hot-plug-hot-power/design/A7-test-B-validation-2026-05-29.md) as validation of A8's intended observability use cases: Test B's Phase 2/3 had to grep journalctl for tb_egpu [F40b]: fire markers (close-path A7 fire, A6 cycle-2 fire, fast-pass rmmod) precisely because A8's sysfs counters were not on disk; Test B rec #5 notes A8 v2 'would have given us machine-readable Phase 2 + 3 counters via tb_egpu_f40b_fires instead of having to grep journalctl. Boost priority.' These are the concrete monitoring use cases A8's surface is meant to serve.
Update the related-patches / cross-ref front matter to point at the two A7 validation reports as the empirical basis for v2, alongside the existing in-driver-recovery-target design doc reference.

Update the 'Vanilla baseline files' provenance entry to drop the nv-pci.c dev_groups edit and describe the nv.c attribute_group + sysfs_create_group/sysfs_remove_group wiring instead (see requirement revision).

designs.design_variant: A — CONSERVATIVE: a near-verbatim clone of A3's (nv-tb-egpu-recover) / A2's (nv-tb-egpu-qwd) sysfs idiom. A8 v2 carries forward v1's atomic state struct, the 5 DEVICE_ATTR show functions, the NULL-terminated attribute array, and the file-static const attribute_group UNCHANGED; it only swaps the registration mechanism from the broken `pci_driver.driver.dev_groups` field-assignment to explicit `sysfs_create_group`/`sysfs_remove_group` calls invoked from new `tb_egpu_metrics_init`/`tb_egpu_metrics_stop` lifecycle helpers wired into nv_pci_probe / nv_pci_remove_helper alongside A2's QWD calls. Reviewer can lay A3's nv-tb-egpu-recover.c:786-884 next to A8 v2's metrics file and see the same skeleton line-for-line.
designs.file_layout:
Three files patched, mirroring A3's file geometry (dedicated TU + header + two nv-pci.c call sites):

1. NEW kernel-open/nvidia/nv-tb-egpu-metrics.c — the entire A8 implementation moves OUT of nv.c into its own TU, exactly as A3 lives in nv-tb-egpu-recover.c and A2 in nv-tb-egpu-qwd.c. Contains: enum nv_tb_egpu_state; the file-static `nv_tb_egpu_metrics` atomic state struct (carried verbatim from v1); nv_tb_egpu_state_str(); the three mutator hooks nv_tb_egpu_f40b_fired/_recovery_succeeded/_recovery_failed (carried verbatim from v1); the 5 DEVICE_ATTR_RO show functions (carried verbatim from v1); `static struct attribute *tb_egpu_metrics_attrs[]`; `static const struct attribute_group tb_egpu_metrics_attr_group = { .attrs = tb_egpu_metrics_attrs };` (NOTE: renamed from v1's nv_tb_egpu_attr_group to the tb_egpu_*_attr_group naming A2/A3 use; the v1 `nv_tb_egpu_dev_groups[]` wrapper array is DELETED — dev_groups is gone); and the two new lifecycle helpers tb_egpu_metrics_init/_stop.

2. NEW kernel-open/nvidia/nv-tb-egpu-metrics.h — mirrors nv-tb-egpu-recover.h: forward-declares nv_linux_state_t, declares `int tb_egpu_metrics_init(nv_linux_state_t *nvl)` + `void tb_egpu_metrics_stop(nv_linux_state_t *nvl)` (Lifecycle), and re-declares the three A9-facing mutator hooks `void nv_tb_egpu_f40b_fired(void)` / `void nv_tb_egpu_recovery_succeeded(void)` / `void nv_tb_egpu_recovery_failed(int permanent)` so A6's call site and a future A9 can include the header instead of relying on loose externs.

3. PATCH kernel-open/nvidia/nv-pci.c — add `#include "nv-tb-egpu-metrics.h"` next to the existing A2/A3 includes (nv-pci.c:30-31); REMOVE the v1 `extern const struct attribute_group *nv_tb_egpu_dev_groups[];` block and the `.driver.dev_groups = nv_tb_egpu_dev_groups,` line from struct nv_pci_driver; ADD `(void)tb_egpu_metrics_init(nvl);` in nv_pci_probe and `tb_egpu_metrics_stop(nvl);` in nv_pci_remove_helper (see call-site proposals).

4. PATCH kernel-open/nvidia/nvidia-sources.Kbuild — add `NVIDIA_SOURCES += nvidia/nv-tb-egpu-metrics.c` directly below the existing nv-tb-egpu-recover.c line (Kbuild:16), so the new TU compiles. (A3/A2/A1 each have an entry here at Kbuild:14-16.)

5. PATCH kernel-open/nvidia/nv.c — REMOVE the entire A8 v1 implementation block (enum, struct, state_str, three hooks, 5 show fns, attr array, attr_group, dev_groups[] — currently the `@@ -1822,6 +1822,138 @@` insertion). The ONLY thing that stays in nv.c from the v1 hunk is A6's call into the hook: the `nv_tb_egpu_f40b_fired();` line inside nv_open_device_for_nvlfp_bounded — which now resolves via the new header include rather than a same-TU definition. (Confirm nv.c already `#include "nv-tb-egpu-metrics.h"` — if A6's TU is nv.c, add the include there too.)

Net file delta vs A8 v1: nv.c shrinks by ~130 lines (whole block relocated), nv-pci.c loses 5 lines (extern + dev_groups) and gains ~3 (include + 2 call sites), two new files appear, Kbuild gains 1 line.
designs.sysfs_api_used: sysfs_create_group(&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj, &tb_egpu_metrics_attr_group) at init; sysfs_remove_group(&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj, &tb_egpu_metrics_attr_group) at stop. Identical signatures, identical kobj source, identical group-by-value-const-static shape as A3 (nv-tb-egpu-recover.c:836-840 / 862-866) and A2 (nv-tb-egpu-qwd.c:414-418 / 432-436). The DEVICE_ATTR_RO + `struct attribute *[]` + `const struct attribute_group` triad is unchanged from v1; the v1 `dev_groups[]` wrapper array and the `.driver.dev_groups` field assignment are both deleted.
designs.create_call_site_proposal: kernel-open/nvidia/nv-pci.c:~2306 — `(void)tb_egpu_metrics_init(nvl);` immediately after the A2 QWD init at line 2305 (post rm_enable_dynamic_power_management, last step of probe). A8 is observability with no pre-rmInit dependency, so it follows QWD's last-in placement rather than A3's early line-2008 placement; nvl->pci_dev is valid since 1993-1999; (void) cast matches A2/A3 convention; device_lock held across probe gives free serialization.
designs.remove_call_site_proposal: kernel-open/nvidia/nv-pci.c:~2387 — `tb_egpu_metrics_stop(nvl);` immediately before the A2 QWD stop at line 2387 (first teardown action, before tb_egpu_recover_stop at 2394 and any RM teardown). Preserves LIFO vs the init ordering and removes the sysfs surface early in remove; device_lock held across remove.
designs.error_handling_approach: Verbatim A3/A2 fail-soft. tb_egpu_metrics_init does: `if (nvl->pci_dev) { int rc = sysfs_create_group(&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj, &tb_egpu_metrics_attr_group); if (rc != 0) NV_DEV_PRINTF(NV_DBG_INFO, nv, "tb_egpu metrics: sysfs_create_group failed: %d\n", rc); }` then `return 0;` unconditionally — log at NV_DBG_INFO (not NV_DBG_ERRORS), no early return, no state cleanup, exactly as recover.c:836-841 and qwd.c:414-419. The `(void)` cast at the call site discards the return anyway. tb_egpu_metrics_stop is `void`, opens with `if (!nvl) return;`, then `if (nvl->pci_dev) sysfs_remove_group(...)` (belt-and-suspenders pci_dev NULL guard matching A3/A2, even though pci_dev was non-NULL at init), and returns — sysfs_remove_group on a never-created group is a benign no-op. This is a strict improvement over v1: v1's dev_groups path failed silently (device_add_groups(dev, NULL) emitted nothing); v2 gives a logged errno to debug from. The kobj itself (&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj) is NOT NULL-checked separately — the PCI core initializes the embedded struct device kobj before probe, so it is guaranteed non-NULL whenever pci_dev is, matching A3/A2's deliberate omission.
designs.coexistence_with_a3: Both groups attach to the SAME kobject — &nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj, i.e. /sys/bus/pci/devices/<DBDF>/ — and both leave attribute_group.name unset, so all files land flat in that one directory. They coexist by disjoint filename prefix, exactly as A2 and A3 already coexist today: A3 publishes tb_egpu_recover_* (5 attrs), A2 publishes tb_egpu_qwd_* (5 attrs), and A8 v2 adds tb_egpu_state + tb_egpu_f40b_fires + tb_egpu_recovery_count + tb_egpu_recovery_failures + tb_egpu_last_recovery_ns (5 attrs) — no name collision across the three sets. Each is a separate const attribute_group registered by its own sysfs_create_group call, so there is no shared-group ownership and the three init/stop lifecycles are fully independent (A8 init after QWD, A8 stop before QWD; A3 init early, A3 stop last). No locking interaction: device_lock serializes the whole probe/remove, so the three create calls run strictly sequentially. The post-change verification `ls /sys/bus/pci/devices/<DBDF>/ | grep -E '^tb_egpu_'` should show all 15 attrs together. This also satisfies the A8 intent's explicit scope boundary "A8 SHALL NOT modify the existing A3 sysfs attributes."
designs.e1_gating_decision: Do NOT gate on nv->is_external_gpu (E1). Register on every bound device, unconditionally — which is what A3 effectively does (A3 gates only on its feature-enable module param NVreg_TbEgpuRecoverEnable, never on is_external_gpu).
