The A8 intent confirms this is correct and intended: "the kernel handles add/remove on every PCI device nvidia.ko binds (E1-classified or otherwise)... non-eGPU devices show perpetually-zero counters and 'healthy' state, which is correct." The metrics struct is updated only from A6's already-E1-gated F40b timeout path, so a non-eGPU's attrs stay at healthy/0 — the sysfs surface itself being present on a non-eGPU is harmless and matches v1's behavioural contract. CONSERVATIVE note: A8 v1 also did not have a per-feature enable module param the way A3/A2 do (no NVreg_TbEgpuMetricsEnable). To hew to "minimum divergence from v1 behaviour," v2 likewise registers unconditionally with NO enable-flag short-circuit — it does not introduce a new kill-switch param. (If a kill-switch is later wanted for parity with A2/A3, it is a separate additive change, out of scope for this conservative mechanism-swap.)
pros: Reviewer can diff A8 v2's nv-tb-egpu-metrics.c against A3's nv-tb-egpu-recover.c:786-884 and A2's nv-tb-egpu-qwd.c:353-455 and see the same skeleton line-for-line — near-zero review surface, the explicit goal.

Eliminates the v1 root-cause footgun entirely: no .driver.dev_groups field, so __pci_register_driver's `drv->driver.dev_groups = drv->dev_groups` clobber (pci-driver.c:1442) can never null it out again. The bug class is structurally impossible in the new mechanism.
Empirically proven idiom on THIS exact driver — A2 and A3's sysfs_create_group on the identical kobj are live and visible in /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:04:00.0/ right now on aorus.19.
Strict failure-mode improvement: v1 failed silently (device_add_groups(dev,NULL) is a no-op with no dmesg); v2 logs an NV_DBG_INFO errno on create failure, giving a debug breadcrumb.
Dedicated-TU layout honours addon-recarve discipline (one addon per TU, no cross-addon source-side reach) — same posture A3/A2/A1 ship, so the addon-recarve audit grep stays clean.
All five v1 attributes, the atomic state struct, and the three A9-facing hooks carry forward UNCHANGED — the A9 dependency timeline and the A6 wiring are untouched; only the registration site moves.
Co-locating init/stop with A2's QWD calls gives correct ordering for free (device fully bound, device_lock held), no new locking, and LIFO symmetry with QWD teardown.
cons: Two new files (nv-tb-egpu-metrics.c/.h) plus a Kbuild entry — slightly more churn than the absolute-minimum 'one-character .dev_groups fix', though it buys consistency and is the recommended path over the field fix.

Driver code now owns explicit create+remove calls (vs v1's declarative dev_groups) — a future maintainer must keep the two call sites symmetric, but this matches the existing A2/A3 maintenance burden exactly.

A8's backing state is a module-lifetime file-static struct (single-GPU assumption carried from v1), so the FIRST-remove-then-teardown ordering A3 uses to protect a per-pdev kfree is, for A8, only structural symmetry rather than a real race guard — a reviewer must understand it is intentionally vestigial here, not load-bearing.

Relocating the implementation out of nv.c into a new TU enlarges the v1→v2 diff beyond the 'flip the mechanism in place' minimum; a reviewer comparing only nv.c hunks will see a large deletion (mitigated by the 1:1 move into the new file).

Registers on every bound PCI device (no E1 gate) — correct per intent, but means a multi-GPU or mixed-device host would show the flat tb_egpu_* attrs on non-eGPU nvidia devices too (same as v1; acceptable under the single-GPU deployment assumption).
designs.one_line_summary: Clone A3/A2's sysfs idiom: relocate A8 v1's unchanged state+attrs into a dedicated nv-tb-egpu-metrics.{c,h} TU, register via sysfs_create_group/sysfs_remove_group from tb_egpu_metrics_init/_stop wired beside A2's QWD calls in nv_pci_probe/remove, and delete the broken .driver.dev_groups path.
helper_function_signatures: int tb_egpu_metrics_init(nv_linux_state_t *nvl);  /* mirrors tb_egpu_recover_init / tb_egpu_qwd_init: returns 0, calls sysfs_create_group(&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj, &tb_egpu_metrics_attr_group) under an if(nvl->pci_dev) guard, logs NV_DBG_INFO on rc!=0, no enable-flag short-circuit (v1 had none) */

void tb_egpu_metrics_stop(nv_linux_state_t *nvl);  /* mirrors tb_egpu_recover_stop / tb_egpu_qwd_stop: if(!nvl) return; then if(nvl->pci_dev) sysfs_remove_group(&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj, &tb_egpu_metrics_attr_group) FIRST; no kfree (state is module-static) */
void nv_tb_egpu_f40b_fired(void);  /* carried verbatim from A8 v1; now declared in nv-tb-egpu-metrics.h so A6's call site in nv.c resolves via include */
void nv_tb_egpu_recovery_succeeded(void);  /* carried verbatim from v1; reserved for A9 */
void nv_tb_egpu_recovery_failed(int permanent);  /* carried verbatim from v1; reserved for A9 */

designs.design_variant: Variant B — IMPROVED. Same surface contract as Variant A (5 read-only per-PCI-device attrs: tb_egpu_state, tb_egpu_f40b_fires, tb_egpu_recovery_count, tb_egpu_recovery_failures, tb_egpu_last_recovery_ns; same A9 hook symbols nv_tb_egpu_f40b_fired/recovery_succeeded/recovery_failed; same A6 wiring at the timeout path), but the failing pci_driver.driver.dev_groups registration is replaced with A3's proven explicit sysfs_create_group-at-probe / sysfs_remove_group-at-remove pattern, and the implementation is restructured for cleaner separation of concerns and better error propagation. Two new helpers split "register the attribute group" from "initialise the counter/state storage" so test code (and A9) can drive each independently. dev_groups was empirically falsified on this driver (A7-test-A-validation-2026-05-29.md line 163: symbols present in kallsyms but zero sysfs paths materialised), so Variant B is the only one that actually produces the surface the contract promises.
designs.file_layout:
Two files, mirroring A8 v1's footprint but inverting WHERE the registration lives.

1. kernel-open/nvidia/nv.c — owns ALL of A8 v2 EXCEPT the probe/remove call sites. Keeps the existing v1 block (enum nv_tb_egpu_state, the global nv_tb_egpu_metrics struct, nv_tb_egpu_state_str, the three A9 hooks nv_tb_egpu_f40b_fired/recovery_succeeded/recovery_failed, the five DEVICE_ATTR_RO show fns, nv_tb_egpu_attrs[], nv_tb_egpu_attr_group). REMOVES the v1 dev_groups export array (const struct attribute_group *nv_tb_egpu_dev_groups[]). ADDS two new exported lifecycle helpers nv_tb_egpu_attrs_init() and nv_tb_egpu_attrs_teardown() (see signatures) plus an internal nv_tb_egpu_metrics_reset(). The A6 wiring (nv_tb_egpu_f40b_fired() call in nv_open_device_for_nvlfp_bounded timeout path, applied-tree nv.c:2041) is UNCHANGED.

2. kernel-open/nvidia/nv-pci.c — REMOVES the v1 extern decl of nv_tb_egpu_dev_groups[] (applied-tree line 3045) and the .driver.dev_groups field assignment in nv_pci_driver (line 3061). ADDS an extern decl of the two new helpers near the top, and call sites: nv_tb_egpu_attrs_init(nvl) in nv_pci_probe right after A3's tb_egpu_recover_check_wpr2_at_probe (after line 2016), and nv_tb_egpu_attrs_teardown(nvl) in nv_pci_remove_helper right after tb_egpu_recover_stop(nvl) (after line 2394).

No new .c/.h files and no nvidia-sources.Kbuild change — unlike A3 (which is a whole subsystem in its own TU), A8 v2 stays inline in nv.c exactly like v1, so the Kbuild and the build graph are untouched. Net file count vs v1: identical (nv.c + nv-pci.c). Intent doc docs/patch-intents/A8-f40b-sysfs-observability.md is bumped to v2 separately (task #275): the requirement currently FORBIDDING explicit sysfs_create_group is inverted, the mechanism description switches to probe-time registration, and the A8 v1 dev_groups failure is recorded as provenance.
designs.sysfs_api_used: sysfs_create_group(&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj, &nv_tb_egpu_attr_group) at probe; sysfs_remove_group(&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj, &nv_tb_egpu_attr_group) at remove. Identical primitive and identical kobject target as A3-recovery (applied tree nv-tb-egpu-recover.c:836/864). Keeps DEVICE_ATTR_RO + struct attribute_group from v1 (the attribute *definitions* were fine; only the registration *mechanism* was broken). sysfs_emit retained in the show functions. The pci_driver.driver.dev_groups mechanism is dropped entirely.
designs.create_call_site_proposal: kernel-open/nvidia/nv-pci.c in nv_pci_probe, immediately after A3's probe-time WPR2 check (after applied-tree line 2016, i.e. after the tb_egpu_recover_check_wpr2_at_probe(...) call). Rationale: (1) This is the same addon block where A3 already registers its sysfs group via tb_egpu_recover_init, so A8 v2's attrs land adjacent to A3's — both surfaces appear/disappear together at the same probe/remove boundary, which is exactly the coexistence the intent wants. (2) pci_set_drvdata(pci_dev, nvl) has already run (line 1993), so to_pci_dev(dev)->drvdata resolves in the show functions and nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj is a fully-formed device kobject — sysfs_create_group will actually materialise paths (the precise failure mode dev_groups hit). (3) It is BEFORE rm_init_private_state (line 2039), so this is intentionally NOT gated on E1 — see e1_gating_decision. Placing it here (rather than later in probe) also means the surface exists even if a later probe step fails, giving post-mortem tooling a counter surface on a half-probed device.
designs.remove_call_site_proposal: kernel-open/nvidia/nv-pci.c in nv_pci_remove_helper, immediately after tb_egpu_recover_stop(nvl) (after applied-tree line 2394). Rationale: symmetric teardown with the A3-adjacent create site, and it runs while nvl->pci_dev is still valid (pci_set_drvdata(...NULL) happens later at line 2557 in the outer remove). Ordering after A3's stop mirrors how A2/A3 teardowns are sequenced and keeps the addon block contiguous. nv_tb_egpu_attrs_teardown is idempotent (guards on a registered flag), so a probe that bailed before the create call still has a safe no-op teardown — closing the asymmetric-cleanup hole that an unconditional sysfs_remove_group would risk.
designs.error_handling_approach: Improved over both v1 (which had NO error handling — dev_groups silently no-op'd) and over a naive copy of A3. nv_tb_egpu_attrs_init returns int and propagates sysfs_create_group's rc to the caller. Policy: log-and-continue, device bind does NOT fail. Rationale: A8 is telemetry-tier `nominal`; losing the observability surface must never take down a working GPU (matching A3's own 'failure is non-fatal' comment at nv-tb-egpu-recover.c). The nv_pci_probe call site uses (void)nv_tb_egpu_attrs_init(nvl) — same (void)-cast convention as the adjacent A3 calls — so the returned rc is available for unit tests / future callers but the probe path explicitly ignores it. On failure the helper emits NV_DEV_PRINTF(NV_DBG_ERRORS,...) (A3 uses NV_DBG_INFO; Variant B upgrades to NV_DBG_ERRORS because a SILENT missing surface is exactly the v1 bug we are fixing — an operator who expected counters and got none deserves a log line) and sets nvl->tb_egpu_attrs_registered = false so teardown skips the remove. Separation point: nv_tb_egpu_attrs_init calls nv_tb_egpu_metrics_reset() (zero the counters / state=healthy) BEFORE sysfs_create_group, so even on registration failure the in-kernel counters are valid and the A6/A9 hooks still work — the data plane survives a control-plane (sysfs) failure. A registered-flag (bool nvl->tb_egpu_attrs_registered, added to nv_linux_state_t next to A3's `recover` pointer) makes teardown idempotent and prevents a double sysfs_remove_group or a remove on a group that never registered.
designs.coexistence_with_a3: Clean and explicit. Both A3 and A8 v2 call sysfs_create_group on the SAME kobject (&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj) but with DIFFERENT, non-overlapping attribute_groups (A3: tb_egpu_recover_attr_group with tb_egpu_recover_fires/_successes/_surrenders/_last_fire_jiffies/_force_trigger; A8: nv_tb_egpu_attr_group with tb_egpu_state/_f40b_fires/_recovery_count/_recovery_failures/_last_recovery_ns). The kernel sysfs core supports multiple unnamed groups on one kobject as long as attribute names are unique across them — and they are (A3 prefixes tb_egpu_recover_*, A8 prefixes tb_egpu_* without the recover_ infix, no collisions). Lifecycle is parallel and independent: A3 registers in tb_egpu_recover_init (probe ~line 2008) and removes in tb_egpu_recover_stop (remove ~line 2394); A8 v2 registers in nv_tb_egpu_attrs_init right after (probe ~line 2016) and removes in nv_tb_egpu_attrs_teardown right after A3's stop (remove ~line 2394+). Critically, A8's storage is a module-global nv_tb_egpu_metrics struct (single-GPU assumption, per the intent), whereas A3's storage is per-device in nvl->recover — so there is zero shared mutable state between the two surfaces; they cannot corrupt each other's counters. A8 v2 does NOT touch, read, or depend on any A3 symbol, and A3 does not know A8 exists. The A9 forthcoming patch is where the two converge (A9 increments A8's recovery_count/failures and shares A3's pre_schedule_gates), but A8 v2 itself stays independent. Note one deliberate DIVERGENCE from A3: A8 v2 does NOT add a force_trigger write attribute (per the v1 intent Scope boundary — F40b fires from a timeout, not a userspace poke), so the surfaces stay clearly differentiated by both name and direction (A8 is read-only; A3 has the one 0200 write attr).
designs.e1_gating_decision: Do NOT gate registration on nv->is_external_gpu (E1) — register on EVERY bound device, exactly as A3 does for its own sysfs group. Three reasons, the first decisive: (1) TIMING — nv->is_external_gpu is classified inside rm_init_private_state at nv-pci.c:2039, which runs AFTER the A3-adjacent create site at ~line 2016. At the natural registration point the E1 flag is not yet reliably set, so gating there would mis-classify the eGPU as non-eGPU and skip the surface on the very device we care about. (2) CONTRACT — the v1 intent explicitly states the kernel should create attrs on every device nvidia.ko binds, and that non-eGPU devices showing perpetually 'healthy' + zero counters 'is correct' (intent Requirement 1 + Scenario 'F40b fire on non-eGPU still updates global counter'). The data plane is already E1-safe by construction: the only writer that changes state, nv_tb_egpu_f40b_fired(), is called from A6's timeout path which IS gated on nv->is_external_gpu at nv.c:1997 — so a non-eGPU never reaches the writer and its attrs stay 'healthy'/0 without any registration-time gate. (3) CONSISTENCY — A3 registers unconditionally and gates only the recovery ACTION (via NVreg_TbEgpuRecoverEnable), not the observability surface. A8 v2 mirrors that: register the surface always, let the gated writer (A6, and later A9 which DOES gate on is_external_gpu at runtime) decide what gets recorded. This keeps A8 v2 a pure observability layer with no policy. Contrast: A9 legitimately gates its recovery scheduling on is_external_gpu because that runs in the open() path long after classification; A8's registration cannot and should not.
pros: Actually materialises the sysfs surface on this driver — the entire point. dev_groups was empirically proven non-functional here (A7-test-A-validation line 163: kallsyms had the symbols, /sys had nothing); sysfs_create_group is proven working by A3's identical, shipping registration.

Separation of concerns: nv_tb_egpu_attrs_init (register attrs) is split from nv_tb_egpu_metrics_reset (init counters/state). Test code and A9 can reset the data plane without touching sysfs, and can register/teardown the control plane without resetting counters. v1 had no such seam — everything was implicit in the dev_groups field.

Better error propagation: helper returns rc, logs at NV_DBG_ERRORS on failure (v1 was silent), but bind never fails — telemetry loss can't kill a working GPU. The data plane (counters + A6/A9 hooks) survives a sysfs registration failure because metrics_reset runs before sysfs_create_group.

Idempotent, asymmetry-safe teardown via an explicit nvl->tb_egpu_attrs_registered flag — a probe that bails before registration gets a safe no-op teardown; no risk of sysfs_remove_group on an unregistered group.
Surfaces appear/disappear in lockstep with A3's surface at the same probe/remove boundary, so operators and tooling see one coherent add/remove event for all tb_egpu_* attrs.
Zero new files, zero Kbuild change, zero new module params — strictly smaller blast radius than A3-style (which is a full TU). Stays inline in nv.c like v1.

No surface-contract change: same 5 attrs, same A9 hook symbols, same A6 wiring — only the registration mechanism and internal structure change, so A9's draft intent (which already names these symbols) needs no revision.
cons: Re-introduces explicit probe/remove lifecycle code that v1's dev_groups was meant to avoid — slightly more code and two more call sites to keep in sync vs the (broken) declarative one-liner. Mitigated by following A3's exact, already-reviewed pattern.

Adds one field (bool tb_egpu_attrs_registered) to nv_linux_state_t — a per-device field backing a global metrics struct, a minor model mismatch. Justified because registration IS per-device even though the metrics are global; the flag tracks whether THIS device's kobject got the group.

Still single-GPU (global nv_tb_egpu_metrics) — unchanged from v1; multiple bound devices would each register the group but share one counter set. Acceptable per project single-eGPU posture and the intent's stated assumption; flagged for the eventual multi-GPU refactor (same caveat A3 carries).

Registration is NOT E1-gated, so a second non-eGPU NVIDIA card (if ever present) would also get tb_egpu_* attrs reading healthy/0. Harmless and contract-sanctioned, but a reader unaware of the design might expect eGPU-only attrs. Documented in the v2 intent.
Minor divergence from A3 in log level (NV_DBG_ERRORS vs A3's NV_DBG_INFO on sysfs failure) — intentional but creates a small inconsistency between two sibling surfaces' failure logging.
helper_function_signatures: int nv_tb_egpu_attrs_init(nv_linux_state_t *nvl); /* resets metrics, then sysfs_create_group on nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj; sets nvl->tb_egpu_attrs_registered; returns 0 or sysfs rc (non-fatal to caller) */
void nv_tb_egpu_attrs_teardown(nv_linux_state_t *nvl); /* idempotent: if nvl->tb_egpu_attrs_registered, sysfs_remove_group and clear the flag; safe on a never-registered or NULL-pci_dev nvl */

static void nv_tb_egpu_metrics_reset(void); /* zero f40b_fires/recovery_count/recovery_failures/last_recovery_ns; state := NV_TB_EGPU_STATE_HEALTHY — the 'init counters' half, callable independently of sysfs by test code */
designs.one_line_summary: A8 v2 Variant B swaps the empirically-broken pci_driver.driver.dev_groups for A3's proven sysfs_create_group-at-probe pattern, splitting attr-registration from counter-init for testability and adding non-fatal rc propagation, while keeping v1's exact surface contract and registering unconditionally (E1 is unclassified at the probe-time create site).

designs.design_variant: Variant C — SHARED-PRIMITIVE. Factor a tiny `tb_egpu_sysfs_register()` / `tb_egpu_sysfs_unregister()` pair into the EXISTING A1-owned shared TU (`kernel-open/nvidia/nv-tb-egpu-pcie.{c,h}`), and convert A2's qwd, A3's recover, AND A8 v2's f40b surfaces to all call it. VERDICT: worth it, but ONLY because a shared tb-egpu primitive file (A1's nv-tb-egpu-pcie.c) already exists and is already consumed by A2+A3 — so the indirection adds a function, not a new file or a new ownership relationship. If A1 did NOT already own a shared TU, I would judge this premature and recommend Variant A (A8 v2 inlines the A3/A2 sysfs_create_group pattern, no refactor). Net is borderline-positive, NOT a slam dunk; see cons.
designs.file_layout:
A8 v2 (this patch) touches exactly two files and converts a third site for free:
1. kernel-open/nvidia/nv-tb-egpu-pcie.h (A1-owned) — add prototypes for `tb_egpu_sysfs_register`/`tb_egpu_sysfs_unregister`. ~6 LOC.
2. kernel-open/nvidia/nv-tb-egpu-pcie.c (A1-owned) — add the two helper bodies (~24 LOC).
3. kernel-open/nvidia/nv.c — A8 v2's own delta: define `nv_tb_egpu_attr_group` + the 5 DEVICE_ATTR_RO show fns (identical to v1's attr definitions, KEPT) + add `tb_egpu_recover`-style `nv_tb_egpu_sysfs_init(nvl)`/`nv_tb_egpu_sysfs_stop(nvl)` that call the shared helper, and wire those into nv_pci_probe / nv_pci_remove_helper.
4. kernel-open/nvidia/nv-pci.c — REMOVE the v1 `extern ... nv_tb_egpu_dev_groups[]` decl and the `.driver.dev_groups = nv_tb_egpu_dev_groups` line (v1's failing mechanism). ~2 LOC removed.
IMPORTANT — Variant C does NOT re-edit A2's or A3's .patch in this A8 v2 deliverable. A8 v2 ships standalone. Converting A2/A3's existing two call sites to the new helper is a SEPARATE follow-on patch (call it A1-D2 / a paired-cascade refactor) because A2 and A3 are already-shipped, soaked, in-manifest addons; rewriting their TUs to route through a new helper is a force-push cascade event that must be its own reviewed change under the cascade carve-out, not smuggled into A8 v2. So the "all three call it" end-state is reached in TWO steps: (step 1) A8 v2 introduces the helper in A1's TU and is the first/only caller; (step 2) a follow-on flips A2+A3 to the helper. This keeps A8 v2's blast radius to nv.c + nv-pci.c + 2 additive lines in A1's shared files.
designs.sysfs_api_used: sysfs_create_group / sysfs_remove_group on &nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj — the exact API A2 (qwd) and A3 (recover) already use. The helper is a thin wrapper: `int tb_egpu_sysfs_register(nv_linux_state_t *nvl, const struct attribute_group *group)` calls sysfs_create_group; `void tb_egpu_sysfs_unregister(nv_linux_state_t *nvl, const struct attribute_group *group)` calls sysfs_remove_group. Abandons v1's pci_driver.driver.dev_groups, which task #273 records as having failed to materialise the attributes (the dev_groups field on a PCI driver bound via the legacy/probe path did not produce the files on this driver's bind sequence).
designs.create_call_site_proposal: kernel-open/nvidia/nv.c ~line 2010 (nv_pci_probe, immediately after the A3-introduced `(void)tb_egpu_recover_init(nvl);`): add `(void)nv_tb_egpu_sysfs_init(nvl);`. Rationale below in error_handling/create fields — probe is the one site where nvl->pci_dev is populated and where A2/A3 already register, so co-locating keeps a single registration locus.
designs.remove_call_site_proposal: kernel-open/nvidia/nv-pci.c: remove v1's `extern ... *nv_tb_egpu_dev_groups[];` (~line 50) and `.driver.dev_groups = nv_tb_egpu_dev_groups,` (~line 59); remove v1's `nv_tb_egpu_dev_groups[]` array in nv.c (~lines 197-201). Add `nv_tb_egpu_sysfs_stop(nvl);` in nv_pci_remove_helper (nv.c) right after `tb_egpu_recover_stop(nvl);` (~line 2392), unregistering before any state teardown, mirroring A2/A3 ordering.
designs.error_handling_approach: Identical to A2/A3 precedent and non-negotiable for parity: register returns int from sysfs_create_group; the caller casts to void and the helper itself emits a single NV_DEV_PRINTF(NV_DBG_INFO/_ERRORS) on non-zero rc. Sysfs failure is NON-FATAL — the f40b counters simply aren't visible, but the driver and the F40b bounded-wait path keep working. The helper guards `if (!nvl || !nvl->pci_dev) return -EINVAL;` (A2/A3 guard nvl->pci_dev at the call site; folding that guard INTO the helper is one of the small wins of centralising). unregister is void and idempotent (sysfs_remove_group is safe to call even if create failed/partial). nv_tb_egpu_sysfs_init also short-circuits if a future enable-toggle is 0 (currently A8 has no enable param; if A5-style toggles add one, the gate lives in the nv.c wrapper, NOT the shared helper — the helper stays policy-free).
designs.coexistence_with_a3: Clean coexistence, no overlap. Three INDEPENDENT attribute_groups register against the SAME device kobj (&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj): A2's tb_egpu_qwd_attr_group (tb_egpu_qwd_*), A3's tb_egpu_recover_attr_group (tb_egpu_recover_*), and A8 v2's nv_tb_egpu_attr_group (tb_egpu_state, tb_egpu_f40b_fires, tb_egpu_recovery_count/_failures, tb_egpu_last_recovery_ns). All three groups use `.name = NULL` (flat attrs on the device dir, not a subdirectory), and sysfs_create_group happily merges multiple NULL-name groups onto one kobj as long as the attribute NAMES are disjoint — they are (qwd_*/recover_*/state+f40b+recovery_* prefixes never collide). Each surface owns its own lifecycle helper and its own create/remove call, so the shared register() primitive does NOT centralise OWNERSHIP — it only centralises the 3-line MECHANICS. A3's recover surface is completely untouched by A8 v2 (the A3 .patch is not re-edited in this deliverable). The intent doc's existing "A8 SHALL NOT modify the existing A3 sysfs attributes" boundary is preserved verbatim.
pros: A shared tb-egpu primitive TU ALREADY EXISTS (A1's nv-tb-egpu-pcie.c, established for WPR2/AER reads consumed by A2+A3) — so the helper is +1 function in an existing file, not a new file or a new dependency edge. This is the single fact that flips the verdict from 'premature' to 'worth it'.

Centralises the nvl->pci_dev NULL-guard + the sysfs_create_group rc-warn boilerplate that is currently copy-pasted verbatim in 2 sites (A2 qwd_init, A3 recover_init) and would become 3 with A8 v2. One place to fix if the kobj target or the warn-level convention ever changes.
A8 v2 itself stays tiny and self-contained: nv.c wrapper + nv-pci.c removal + 2 additive lines in A1's shared files. Low blast radius for THIS patch.
Directly fixes the v1 dev_groups defect (task #273) by adopting the empirically-proven sysfs_create_group path that A2/A3 have soaked on aorus.15.
The helper is policy-free (no enable-gate, no E1 logic inside it) so it generalises cleanly to a future 4th surface without accreting branches.
cons: The task's proposed signature tb_egpu_sysfs_register(nvl, group, name) carries a `name` param the existing call sites do NOT need — both A2 and A3 use flat (.name=NULL) groups. Adding `name` is speculative generality (YAGNI); the honest helper is tb_egpu_sysfs_register(nvl, group) with no name. Shipping the 3-arg version would itself be premature abstraction.

The wrapped body is THREE lines (guard + create + warn). Wrapping 3 lines behind a cross-TU function call + a header prototype is close to the floor where indirection costs more reading-cost than it saves — a reviewer now has to jump to nv-tb-egpu-pcie.c to confirm the registration semantics that were previously inline and obvious.

Reaching the 'all three call it' end-state REQUIRES touching the already-shipped, soaked A2 and A3 patches, which triggers a force-push cascade on the a2-*/a3-* fork branches. That is real process cost (lease-push, range-diff, catalog update, user-confirm per the carve-out) for a 3-line dedup. Step-2 may simply never be worth executing, leaving the helper with a single caller (A8) — at which point the abstraction has NEGATIVE value (it's indirection serving one consumer).

Cross-addon coupling direction: A8 (a leaf observability addon) would depend on A1 (core primitives) for sysfs mechanics it could trivially inline. That's an extra manifest-order/build dependency to honour for marginal benefit.

A8 v1's intent doc currently FORBIDS sysfs_create_group; Variant C (like A/B) needs that requirement rewritten anyway (task #275). The shared-primitive framing adds doc surface (must document the A1-ownership of the helper) without changing that obligation.
designs.one_line_summary: Variant C is worth it ONLY because A1 already owns a shared tb-egpu TU; add a policy-free tb_egpu_sysfs_register(nvl, group) (drop the speculative `name` arg), make A8 v2 its first caller via sysfs_create_group at probe, and defer the A2/A3 conversion to a separate cascade-gated follow-on — but if you want minimum blast radius and zero cascade risk, Variant A (inline the proven A3 pattern in A8 v2) is the safer default and I'd pick it unless the team commits to actually completing the A2/A3 conversion.
designs.e1_gating_decision: Do NOT gate on nv->is_external_gpu (E1), matching A2 and A3 exactly. Neither qwd_init nor recover_init checks is_external_gpu; they gate only on their own NVreg enable param and register on every bound device. A8 v1's intent doc explicitly specifies the surface appears on EVERY bound PCI device (E1-classified or not) and that non-eGPU devices simply show perpetually-zero counters / 'healthy' state, which is correct because the only mutator (nv_tb_egpu_f40b_fired) is reached solely from A6's E1-gated timeout path. So: always register, never E1-gate. The is_external_gpu signal is set during rm_init_adapter (well after probe) and is not reliably available at the probe-time registration point anyway — another reason A2/A3 don't use it and A8 v2 must not.
helper_function_signatures: /* A8 v2-local wrappers in nv.c, NOT in the shared TU: */ static int nv_tb_egpu_sysfs_init(nv_linux_state_t *nvl);

verdicts.lens: CORRECTNESS — would each design actually materialise the 5 sysfs attributes at /sys/bus/pci/devices/<DBDF>/, free of races, NULL hazards, and lifecycle (attr-before-kobj / attr-after-free / unbind-rebind) bugs? Verified against the live A3 (nv-tb-egpu-recover.c:766-884) and A2 (nv-tb-egpu-qwd.c:350-455) idioms, the nv-pci.c probe/remove call sites (drvdata @1993, A3 init @2008, rm_init_private_state/is_external_gpu @2039, QWD init @2305, QWD stop @2387, A3 stop @2394), the nv_linux_state_t fields (nv-linux.h:1255/1445/1453), and the dev_groups clobber in the kernel itself (linux-v6.19/drivers/pci/pci-driver.c:1442 `drv->driver.dev_groups = drv->dev_groups;`).

scores.design_variant: A — CONSERVATIVE (clone A3/A2 idiom verbatim into dedicated nv-tb-egpu-metrics.{c,h} TU, swap dev_groups for sysfs_create_group/sysfs_remove_group)
strengths: Will actually produce the 5 attrs at the expected path. sysfs_create_group(&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj, &group) is the exact, verified-live primitive A2 (nv-tb-egpu-qwd.c:414) and A3 (nv-tb-egpu-recover.c:836) use; same kobj (/sys/bus/pci/devices/<DBDF>/), same flat-group shape (.name unset), same DEVICE_ATTR_RO triad. Correctness of materialisation is empirically established on this driver.

Root-cause diagnosis is the PRECISE one and I verified it: kernel pci-driver.c:1442 unconditionally does `drv->driver.dev_groups = drv->dev_groups`, so v1 setting the inner struct-device-driver `.driver.dev_groups` is overwritten at __pci_register_driver time by the outer pci_driver.dev_groups (which v1 left NULL) → silent no-op. Moving to sysfs_create_group makes that bug class structurally impossible. Design A is the only variant that names this mechanism correctly.

No attr-before-kobj hazard: create runs at the QWD site (~2306), well after drvdata @1993 and pci_dev assignment @1999, so &nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj is a fully-initialised embedded device kobj. The PCI core guarantees the embedded kobj is live before probe, so the deliberately-omitted explicit kobj NULL-check is sound (matches A3/A2).

No attr-after-free / reader-vs-kfree race for A8 specifically: A8's backing store is a module-lifetime file-static struct that is NEVER kfree'd, so even though stop removes sysfs FIRST (vestigial LIFO symmetry), there is no per-device allocation a late reader could dangle against. The honest cons note correctly flags this ordering as vestigial-not-load-bearing.

device_lock held across probe/remove serialises all three create/remove calls — no inter-addon locking needed, no concurrent-bind race on the same device, exactly inherited from the A2/A3 call-site placement.
Strict failure-visibility improvement over v1: logs an NV_DBG_INFO errno on create failure instead of v1's totally silent device_add_groups(dev,NULL) no-op.
weaknesses: UNBIND/REBIND (DaemonSet pod restart) CORRECTNESS GAP: A8's counters are module-global and Design A carries v1's state 'UNCHANGED' with the helper explicitly doing 'no state cleanup' / no reset-on-init. On rebind the same global struct is re-exposed against the new kobj carrying STALE counters from the previous bind generation (f40b_fires, recovery_count, last_recovery_ns all non-zero from the prior life). The attrs materialise correctly but read wrong values after a rebind cycle — a real observability-correctness bug under exactly the DaemonSet-restart scenario the prompt calls out. Mitigable with one line (reset in init) but Design A does not specify it.

Show functions inherited 'verbatim from v1' must resolve backing state. A3's show fns deref pci_get_drvdata→nvl->recover (per-device); A8's read a module-global directly. If the carried-over v1 show fns still call to_pci_dev(dev)/pci_get_drvdata, that path is fine here (drvdata set @1993 before any create); but Design A does not state the resolution mechanism, leaving an unverified assumption about whether the global is read directly (safe) vs via a drvdata indirection that during a torn-down rebind window could briefly be NULL (the show fns would need the same `if(!nvl)` guard A3 has). The design does not surface this.

Module-global state means a 2nd bound NVIDIA device re-registers the SAME global group on its own kobj — correct contract-wise, but both kobjs' attrs alias one counter set; not a crash, but a multi-device correctness ambiguity carried silently from v1.

scores.design_variant: B — IMPROVED (sysfs_create_group inline in nv.c like v1, split nv_tb_egpu_attrs_init / nv_tb_egpu_metrics_reset, rc propagation, idempotent teardown via nvl->tb_egpu_attrs_registered flag)
strengths: Materialises the surface via the same proven sysfs_create_group on &nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj — same correctness floor as A.

SOLVES the rebind-stale-counter bug that A and C leave open: nv_tb_egpu_metrics_reset() runs at the start of every nv_tb_egpu_attrs_init, BEFORE sysfs_create_group, so each (re)bind generation starts at state=HEALTHY / counters=0. This is the only design that makes the DaemonSet pod-restart cycle read correct values. Decisive for the correctness lens.

Idempotent, asymmetry-safe teardown via explicit per-device bool nvl->tb_egpu_attrs_registered: a probe that bailed before the create call gets a safe no-op teardown, and a double-remove (e.g. error-path remove then normal remove) cannot call sysfs_remove_group on an unregistered group. Closes a real lifecycle hazard that an unconditional sysfs_remove_group would carry. (A/C rely on sysfs_remove_group-on-never-created being a benign no-op, which is true, but B's flag also guards the registered-bit semantics more explicitly.)

data-plane-survives-control-plane-failure: metrics_reset before sysfs_create_group means the A6 f40b hook and A9 counters keep correct in-kernel state even if sysfs registration fails — the counters are valid the instant probe returns, independent of sysfs success.

E1-gate timing reasoning is correct and I verified it: B's create site (~line 2016, right after A3's WPR2 check) is BEFORE rm_init_private_state @2039 where is_external_gpu is classified, so B correctly refuses to gate on E1 there (gating would mis-skip the eGPU). The data plane stays E1-safe because the only writer (nv_tb_egpu_f40b_fired) is already E1-gated upstream in A6.
No attr-before-kobj hazard (create after drvdata@1993) and surfaces appear/disappear in lockstep with A3 at the same probe/remove boundary — one coherent add/remove uevent for all tb_egpu_* attrs.
weaknesses: The new bool tb_egpu_attrs_registered is per-device but backs a module-GLOBAL metrics struct — a model mismatch B itself flags. Correct (registration IS per-kobj) but a reader must understand the flag tracks THIS device's group, not the counters. Not a bug, but a comprehension hazard.
Inline-in-nv.c (vs A's dedicated TU) means a reviewer can't lay it 1:1 against A3; mild addon-recarve-discipline divergence (style, not correctness).

Upgrading the failure log to NV_DBG_ERRORS diverges from A3's NV_DBG_INFO — defensible (a silent missing surface WAS the v1 bug) but creates a sibling-surface inconsistency; purely cosmetic, no correctness impact.

Still module-global counters → same multi-device aliasing ambiguity as A/C (each device's group reads the one shared counter set). B does not fix the single-GPU assumption, only the rebind-reset symptom; correct under the project's single-eGPU posture.

scores.design_variant: C — SHARED-PRIMITIVE (add policy-free tb_egpu_sysfs_register/unregister into A1's existing nv-tb-egpu-pcie.c, A8 v2 is first caller; defer A2/A3 conversion to a separate cascade)
strengths: Uses the identical sysfs_create_group/sysfs_remove_group primitive, just behind a thin wrapper in an already-existing shared TU — so materialisation correctness is the same proven floor as A/B, and the wrapper folds the nvl/nvl->pci_dev NULL-guard inline (a small, real robustness centralisation).
Correctly diagnoses and removes the v1 dev_groups mechanism (extern + .driver.dev_groups field deletion), so the fix is real.

Honest self-assessment: the design itself states that if A1 didn't already own a shared TU it would be premature, and that Variant A is the safer default — this candor reduces the risk of mis-adoption.

Coexistence analysis is correct: three independent attribute_groups on one kobj with disjoint name prefixes merge cleanly; the shared register() centralises only the 3-line mechanics, not group ownership — so no shared-mutable-state hazard is introduced between A2/A3/A8.
E1-gate decision is correct (always register, never gate) and matches A2/A3.
weaknesses: INHERITS THE SAME REBIND-STALE-COUNTER BUG as A: the nv.c wrappers 'call the shared helper' with NO metrics-reset specified, and state is module-global carried from v1, so a DaemonSet rebind reads stale counters. C explicitly keeps v1's attr definitions and adds only the registration wrapper — the data-plane reset that B has is absent. Under the prompt's unbind/rebind lens this is the same correctness defect as A, with none of B's mitigation.

Cross-addon dependency direction added for marginal gain: a leaf observability addon (A8) now build-depends on A1's TU for 3 lines of sysfs mechanics it could inline. Not a runtime correctness bug, but adds a manifest-ordering constraint and a cross-TU jump that obscures the registration semantics that were previously obvious inline — a comprehension/maintenance correctness drag.

The 'all three call it' end-state requires force-push-cascade-editing already-soaked A2/A3 — process risk that, if never executed (likely for a 3-line dedup), leaves the abstraction with a single caller = NEGATIVE value. The correctness of the SHIPPED A8 v2 doesn't depend on that step, but the design's stated rationale does.

Same idempotent-teardown gap as A: relies on sysfs_remove_group-on-never-created being benign (true) rather than B's explicit registered-flag; no per-device guard against double-remove on error paths.
Same multi-device global-aliasing ambiguity as A/B.

verdicts.composite_recommendation:
Adopt B's mechanism + lifecycle, but GRAFT two elements from A. On the pure correctness lens B wins decisively for ONE verified reason: it is the only design that resets the module-global counters at the start of every (re)bind via nv_tb_egpu_metrics_reset() called before sysfs_create_group. I confirmed A8's state is module-global (all three designs say so) and that A3-style backing state is per-device (nvl->recover); A and C carry v1's state 'unchanged' with explicit 'no state cleanup', so under the DaemonSet pod-restart / unbind-rebind cycle the prompt specifically asks about, A and C will materialise the 5 attrs correctly but display STALE counters from the prior bind generation — a real observability-correctness bug. B alone fixes it. B also adds the idempotent, asymmetry-safe teardown (nvl->tb_egpu_attrs_registered) that protects double-remove / probe-bailed-before-create paths more explicitly than A/C's reliance on remove-of-never-created being benign. All three share the same correct materialisation floor (verified sysfs_create_group on &nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj — I confirmed the v1 dev_groups failure is real via kernel pci-driver.c:1442 `drv->driver.dev_groups = drv->dev_groups` clobbering v1's NULL outer field) and the same correct no-E1-gate decision (is_external_gpu is classified at nv-pci.c:2039, after B/C's early create site).

GRAFTS to take from A into B: (1) RELOCATE B's implementation OUT of nv.c into a dedicated nv-tb-egpu-metrics.{c,h} TU per A's file layout — this honours addon-recarve discipline (one addon per TU, clean audit grep) and lets a reviewer diff it 1:1 against A3, at zero correctness cost. (2) Keep B's failure log but consider A's NV_DBG_INFO level for sibling-surface consistency with A2/A3 (cosmetic; B's NV_DBG_ERRORS is also defensible). 

TWO MUST-FIX correctness items B must still specify before merge, independent of variant choice: (a) the carried-over v1 show functions MUST read the module-global struct directly OR, if they deref pci_get_drvdata, MUST carry A3's `if (!nvl ...) return -ENODEV;` guard, because during a teardown/rebind window drvdata can be NULL — none of the three designs state the show-fn resolution mechanism explicitly; (b) document that module-global counters alias across multiple bound NVIDIA devices (single-eGPU posture makes this acceptable but it should be an explicit, not silent, assumption). Reject C unless the team firmly commits to executing the A2/A3 cascade conversion; as a single-caller abstraction it adds cross-addon coupling for negative net value and carries A's rebind-stale-counter defect.

verdicts.lens: ROBUSTNESS — failure behavior of sysfs_create_group failure, partial/per-group failure, memory pressure, and concurrent bind/unbind. Grounded against the live tree: A1's nv-tb-egpu-pcie.{c,h} exists (verified, no sysfs code today), A2/A3 use sysfs_create_group on &nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj with NV_DBG_INFO log-and-continue and remove-first-then-free ordering (verified at recover.c:830-883 / qwd.c:406-454), no explicit locking in the addons (device_lock from driver core serializes the whole probe/remove callback), and A8's backing state is module-global single-GPU in all three variants. Key kernel-semantics facts that bound the analysis: (1) sysfs_create_group is atomic-on-failure for a SINGLE group — internal_create_group unwinds already-created attrs and returns an error, so 'some attrs created, some not' is NOT a reachable end-state within one group; partial failure only exists at the per-GROUP granularity (A3 group succeeds, A8 group fails), which all three handle identically and non-fatally. (2) kernfs active-reference draining in sysfs_remove_group guarantees an in-flight show() completes before attr teardown — and since A8's state is module-global and never kfree'd, there is NO use-after-free window for A8 regardless of stop ordering (A8's remove-first ordering is therefore a robustness asset only structurally, not load-bearing). (3) None of the three kalloc per-device state, so all three are MORE memory-pressure-robust than A3 itself (which kzallocs nvl->recover).

scores.design_variant: A — CONSERVATIVE (verbatim A3/A2 sysfs_create_group clone in dedicated nv-tb-egpu-metrics.{c,h} TU)
strengths: sysfs_create_group failure is handled exactly as A2/A3 ship today: logged at NV_DBG_INFO, return 0 unconditionally, bind NEVER refused — the (void) cast at the call site discards rc anyway. Empirically proven non-fatal on this driver, not theory.

Maximum memory-pressure immunity: backing state is a file-static struct with ZERO addon-owned allocation in the init path. There is no kalloc that can fail under pressure (unlike A3's kzalloc'd nvl->recover). Only the kernel-internal GFP_KERNEL allocs inside sysfs_create_group can fail, and that path is the handled one.

Concurrent bind/unbind safe for free: registration sits inside nv_pci_probe / nv_pci_remove_helper under driver-core device_lock (verified: A3/A2 take no explicit locks). Per-device kobjects mean concurrent multi-device binds touch disjoint kobjects.

No use-after-free window: A8 state is module-global and never freed, so a show() racing a remove cannot dereference freed backing store regardless of ordering — strictly safer than A3's per-pdev kfree race that A3 must order around.

Partial-GROUP failure (A3 group OK, A8 group fails) is handled correctly and independently — each group is a separate sysfs_create_group call; sysfs_remove_group on a never-created group is a benign no-op, so teardown stays safe.
Structurally impossible to reintroduce the v1 dev_groups clobber bug — no .driver.dev_groups field exists in the new mechanism.
Strict failure-VISIBILITY improvement over v1: v1 failed silently (device_add_groups(dev,NULL) emits nothing); A logs an errno breadcrumb.
weaknesses: Robustness is INHERITED, not ENGINEERED: A copies A3's idiom verbatim but adds no explicit failure-mode machinery of its own. There is no rc propagation, no registered-flag, no data-plane-before-control-plane sequencing.

Stop-ordering (sysfs remove FIRST) is, by the design's own admission, VESTIGIAL for A8 — it protects nothing because the state is module-global and never kfree'd. A future maintainer who adds per-device allocation behind A8 could reintroduce a teardown-asymmetry/use-after-free hole that A's structure does not actively guard against; the safety is accidental, not structural.
No seam separating 'register the attrs' from 'initialize the counters' — if a future evolution needs the data plane valid before the control plane, A's monolithic init offers no place to enforce it.

Relies on the implicit 'sysfs_remove_group on a never-created group is benign' contract for asymmetric-cleanup safety on early-probe-bail, rather than tracking registration state explicitly. True today, but unstated and fragile under refactor.

scores.design_variant: B — IMPROVED (sysfs_create_group inline in nv.c with metrics-reset-before-create, rc propagation, idempotent registered-flag teardown)
strengths: Only variant that treats every failure mode as first-class. metrics_reset() runs BEFORE sysfs_create_group, so the DATA PLANE (counters + state=healthy + A6/A9 hooks) is guaranteed valid even when the CONTROL PLANE (sysfs) fails — control-plane failure cannot corrupt or block the data plane. This is the strongest robustness invariant of the three.

Idempotent, asymmetry-safe teardown via explicit nvl->tb_egpu_attrs_registered flag: a probe that bails BEFORE the create call gets a guaranteed no-op teardown, and a double-remove is structurally impossible. A and C rely on sysfs_remove_group-on-never-created being benign (true, but implicit); B makes the guard explicit AND future-proofs it against a refactor that adds a real per-device allocation.

sysfs_create_group rc is propagated up (returned int), available to unit tests and future callers, while the probe call site still (void)-casts it so bind NEVER fails — best of both: testable error path AND telemetry-loss-can't-kill-a-working-GPU.

Same memory-pressure immunity as A (file-static metrics struct, no addon kalloc); adds only a non-allocating bool field to nv_linux_state_t, consistent with how A2/A3 already carry per-device pointers there (verified at nv-linux.h:1445/1453).

Concurrent bind/unbind safe via the same device_lock serialization at the probe/remove call sites; per-device registered-flag correctly tracks per-device kobject registration even though metrics are global — the right granularity for the asymmetric-cleanup question.
No new files, no Kbuild change, no cross-TU dependency edge — smallest structural blast radius while still being the most robust. Failure surface stays in two files (nv.c + nv-pci.c).

Upgrading the sysfs-failure log to NV_DBG_ERRORS directly attacks the v1 root cause (a SILENT missing surface) — under the robustness lens, surfacing the failure loudly is the correct call for an observability surface whose entire purpose is being absent-or-present.
weaknesses: Adds a per-device bool backing a module-GLOBAL metrics struct — a minor model mismatch (registration is per-device, metrics are global). Harmless, but a reader must understand the flag tracks THIS kobject's group, not the shared counters.

Inherits the single-GPU global-metrics hazard unchanged: two devices binding concurrently each register their own group safely (disjoint kobjects) but share ONE counter set. Atomic counters mean no crash, but the per-device flag does not fix the SEMANTIC conflation of two devices' counters. Same scope caveat A and C carry; B's per-device flag merely makes the boundary visible.

NV_DBG_ERRORS vs A3/A2's NV_DBG_INFO is a deliberate sibling-surface inconsistency — defensible under the robustness lens but a small failure-logging divergence between three surfaces that otherwise behave identically.

Slightly more code (rc propagation + flag + reset seam, ~35 LOC vs A's ~15) and two call sites plus a field to keep in sync — more moving parts than A's verbatim clone, though each part earns its place under this lens.

scores.design_variant: C — SHARED-PRIMITIVE (tb_egpu_sysfs_register/unregister in A1's nv-tb-egpu-pcie.{c,h}; A8 is first caller, A2/A3 conversion deferred)
strengths: Runtime robustness is essentially identical to A: same sysfs_create_group on the same kobject, same log-and-continue non-fatal policy, same module-global no-free state, same device_lock serialization — so the create-fail / partial-group / memory-pressure / concurrent-bind answers are all as safe as A's.

Centralizing the nvl/pci_dev NULL-guard INTO the helper (if (!nvl || !nvl->pci_dev) return -EINVAL) is a genuine robustness improvement over the copy-pasted call-site guards in A2/A3/A — one place to harden, and it makes the unregister path explicitly idempotent (sysfs_remove_group safe after a failed/partial create).
unregister is void + idempotent, matching the benign-no-op semantics the other two rely on, so asymmetric-cleanup on early-probe-bail is safe.

Verified premise holds: A1's nv-tb-egpu-pcie.{c,h} already exists and is consumed by A2/A3, and has NO sysfs code today — so the helper is genuinely additive (+1 policy-free function), not a new ownership relationship or TU.
weaknesses: Introduces a cross-TU failure/dependency edge (A8 leaf observability addon → A1 core primitives) for THREE lines of mechanics. Under the robustness lens this widens the surface a reviewer must reason about for the registration semantics — previously inline and obvious — for no runtime-robustness gain over A.

Leaves the codebase in a HALF-CONVERTED state: the 'shared' primitive has exactly ONE caller (A8) until a separate cascade-gated follow-on flips A2/A3. A shared abstraction serving one consumer has negative value and is fragile-as-a-pattern — and the follow-on may never be executed (force-push cascade cost on already-soaked a2-/a3- branches), stranding it permanently single-caller.

No data-plane-before-control-plane sequencing and no explicit registered-flag — same implicit-benign-no-op reliance as A, so it does not close the asymmetric-cleanup hole that B closes. Robustness machinery is centralized-guard only, not failure-isolation.

Reaching the intended end-state touches already-shipped, soaked A2/A3 patches — a process-robustness risk (cascade, lease-push, range-diff, re-soak) that the runtime gain (3-line dedup) does not justify under this lens.
Module-global single-GPU metrics hazard carried unchanged, same as A and B, with no per-device tracking even at the flag level.

verdicts.composite_recommendation: Adopt B as the winner — it is the only variant that ENGINEERS robustness rather than inheriting it. Three properties decide it under this lens: (1) metrics_reset() before sysfs_create_group guarantees the data plane (counters + A6/A9 hooks) stays valid even when the control plane fails — a control-plane failure can never corrupt or block the observability counters; (2) the explicit nvl->tb_egpu_attrs_registered flag makes teardown idempotent and asymmetric-cleanup-safe by construction, including the early-probe-bail case, and future-proofs against a refactor that adds real per-device allocation behind A8 (where A's and C's implicit 'sysfs_remove_group-on-never-created is benign' reliance would silently break); (3) rc propagation gives a testable error path while the probe site (void)-casts it so bind never fails. All three correctly refuse-to-refuse-binding on create failure, all three are memory-pressure-immune (file-static state, no addon kalloc — strictly safer than A3 itself), all three are concurrent-bind-safe via driver-core device_lock, and 'partial failure' is bounded by kernel semantics to the per-GROUP granularity (handled identically and non-fatally by all three). Graft ONE element from runner-up A into B: keep B's data-plane-before-control-plane and registered-flag machinery, but reconcile the sysfs-failure log level — B chose NV_DBG_ERRORS to defeat the v1 silent-absence bug, A/A3 use NV_DBG_INFO. Recommend NV_DBG_ERRORS (B's choice is correct for an observability surface whose whole failure mode is silent absence) BUT add a one-line comment at the A8 site noting the deliberate divergence from A3/A2's NV_DBG_INFO so the sibling-surface inconsistency is documented rather than accidental. Decline C: its runtime robustness only equals A's while it adds a cross-TU dependency edge and strands a single-caller 'shared' primitive in a half-converted state — process fragility for zero runtime-robustness gain; C only becomes worthwhile if the team firmly commits to executing the A2/A3 conversion follow-on, which is out of scope here. If maximum conservatism is required over engineered robustness (e.g. for an immediate hotfix with minimal review surface), A is the safe fallback — it is maximally proven and memory-pressure-immune — but it carries a self-admitted vestigial teardown that B eliminates.

verdicts.lens: COEXISTENCE + REVIEW-COST. Judged on: (1) does A8 v2 break A3's existing sysfs attributes; (2) how easily the queued v5 deep review can triangulate A8 v2 against A3; (3) how much NEW surface area each variant adds; (4) does each stay minimally divergent from A3 to amortise reviewer cost. Grounded against the live tree: A3 (nv-tb-egpu-recover.c:786 attr_group, :836 create, :864 remove) and A2 (nv-tb-egpu-qwd.c:362/414/434) both attach flat (.name unset) const attribute_groups to &nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj with disjoint tb_egpu_recover_* / tb_egpu_qwd_* prefixes; A8's proposed names (tb_egpu_state, tb_egpu_f40b_fires, tb_egpu_recovery_count/_failures, tb_egpu_last_recovery_ns) collide with NONE of them. Confirmed the A1 shared TU nv-tb-egpu-pcie.{c,h} EXISTS and is consumed by recover/qwd/close/nv-pci, but contains ZERO sysfs code today (its exports are all tb_egpu_pcie_* PCIe-register reads) — so Variant C's premise is real but it would be the FIRST sysfs code added to that shared TU. Also confirmed A8 v1 (the broken dev_groups version) is NOT in the live tree: no nv_tb_egpu_attr_group / DEVICE_ATTR_RO / dev_groups in nv.c or nv-pci.c (only A6's f40b open-work machinery at nv.c:1825+); task #273 has it pending. So the 'v1->v2 diff churn' all three variants discuss is partly moot — nothing is committed to diff against.

scores.design_variant: A — CONSERVATIVE (clone A3/A2 idiom into dedicated nv-tb-egpu-metrics.{c,h} TU)
strengths: COEXISTENCE: clean and verified — disjoint tb_egpu_state/_f40b_fires/_recovery_* prefixes against A3's tb_egpu_recover_* and A2's tb_egpu_qwd_*, same &nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj, flat const attr_group, independent init-after-QWD / stop-before-QWD lifecycle, A3 .patch untouched. Does NOT break A3's attributes; explicitly honours the intent boundary 'A8 SHALL NOT modify the existing A3 sysfs attributes'.

TRIANGULATION: best-in-class. v5 reviewer lays nv-tb-egpu-metrics.c next to nv-tb-egpu-recover.c:786-884 and nv-tb-egpu-qwd.c:353-455 and sees the SAME skeleton line-for-line. The dedicated-TU layout is the exact file geometry A3/A2/A1 already ship (mirror of Kbuild:14-17), so the reviewer's mental model transfers with zero adaptation.

MINIMAL DIVERGENCE: zero behavioral divergence from A3 — same fail-soft (NV_DBG_INFO, return 0 unconditionally, (void) cast at call site), same belt-and-suspenders if(nvl->pci_dev) guard, same sysfs-remove-FIRST teardown order. No new nvl struct field, no new module param, no log-level change. The ONLY semantic change vs the (uncommitted) v1 is the mechanism swap, which is forced.

Structurally eliminates the dev_groups footgun: no .driver.dev_groups field means __pci_register_driver's drv->driver.dev_groups = drv->dev_groups clobber can never null it out again — the bug class becomes impossible, not merely patched.
Honours addon-recarve discipline (one addon per TU, no cross-addon source-side reach) so the recarve audit grep stays clean — same posture as A3/A2/A1.
weaknesses: Adds 2 new files + 1 Kbuild line — more file churn than Design B's stay-in-nv.c, though this churn BUYS the line-for-line triangulability and matches A3's own geometry, so it is net review-POSITIVE not negative.

Relocating A8's body into a new TU enlarges the nominal v1->v2 diff (a large nv.c deletion + new-file addition) — but this is largely moot: v1 was never committed (confirmed: no dev_groups/attr_group in the live tree), so there is no committed v1 hunk to diff against; the reviewer reviews the new TU on its own merits against A3.

Module-lifetime file-static state means A3's sysfs-remove-first ordering is vestigial-symmetry here (no per-pdev kfree to race) rather than load-bearing — reviewer must understand it is intentional mimicry, a one-line comment cost.

Minor cosmetic namespace wrinkle: A8's tb_egpu_state sits in the same flat dir as A3's existing tb_egpu_recover_state — distinct names, no collision, but a reader scanning ls output sees two *_state attrs.

scores.design_variant: B — IMPROVED (stay inline in nv.c, split attrs_init from metrics_reset, add registered-flag, NV_DBG_ERRORS, rc propagation)
strengths: COEXISTENCE: equally clean and verified against A3 — same kobj, disjoint prefixes, independent parallel lifecycle adjacent to A3's, zero shared mutable state (A8 global struct vs A3 per-device nvl->recover), A3 untouched. Does not break A3's attributes.
Zero new files / zero Kbuild change / zero new module param — strictly smaller FILE blast radius than A and C; stays inline in nv.c.
Idempotent asymmetry-safe teardown via the registered-flag closes a real cleanup hole (probe that bails before create gets a safe no-op teardown).
Separating attrs_init (control plane) from metrics_reset (data plane) is genuinely useful for the fake-5090 test-infra and A9 — the data plane survives a sysfs-registration failure.
weaknesses: TRIANGULATION cost is materially higher than A: the v5 reviewer can no longer diff a whole file against A3. The impl is interleaved in nv.c amongst A6's f40b machinery, so the reviewer must mentally extract A8's lines and compare them piecemeal to A3's TU — exactly the work the dedicated-TU layout removes.

DIVERGES from A3 in THREE reviewable ways, each needing justification: (1) log level NV_DBG_ERRORS vs A3's NV_DBG_INFO — a sibling-surface inconsistency the reviewer must adjudicate; (2) a new nvl_linux_state_t field (tb_egpu_attrs_registered) — a per-device bool backing a global metrics struct, a model mismatch flagged in its own cons; (3) rc-propagation seam that the (void) call site immediately discards. Each divergence is defensible but each is NEW surface the v5 pass must reason about rather than pattern-match.

Adding a field to the shared nv_linux_state_s struct is a touch outside A8's own TU — small, but it is exactly the kind of cross-cutting edit the addon-recarve discipline tries to minimise; A3 confines its state to its own struct via the nvl->recover pointer it already owns.
estimated_loc 35 vs A's 15 — the extra LOC is the divergence surface, not bug-fix substance.

The registered-flag's stated benefit (asymmetric-cleanup safety) is partly redundant: sysfs_remove_group on a never-created flat group is already a benign no-op (the exact property A3 relies on by NOT carrying such a flag), so the flag adds a divergence to solve a problem A3 demonstrates does not need solving.

scores.design_variant: C — SHARED-PRIMITIVE (factor tb_egpu_sysfs_register/_unregister into A1's nv-tb-egpu-pcie TU; A8 first caller; defer A2/A3 conversion)
strengths: COEXISTENCE: the eventual sysfs surface is identical to A/B and equally clean against A3 (disjoint prefixes, same kobj, A3 .patch untouched in THIS deliverable). Does not break A3's attributes.
Correctly self-identifies as borderline and recommends Variant A as the safe default unless the team commits to completing the A2/A3 conversion — intellectually honest.
If the deferred cascade DID complete, it would dedup the nvl->pci_dev NULL-guard + rc-warn boilerplate currently copy-pasted in A2 and A3 — a real, if small, future win.
Correctly drops the speculative `name` arg in its own cons (recognises the 3-arg signature as premature generality).
weaknesses: VERIFIED: the A1 shared TU nv-tb-egpu-pcie.c contains ZERO sysfs code today — its entire surface is tb_egpu_pcie_* PCIe-register reads (WPR2/AER/DPC/root-port-walk). Variant C would introduce a brand-new CAPABILITY CLASS (sysfs mechanics) into a TU whose charter is hardware-register access. That is the single largest NEW surface-area add of the three, and the worst fit for amortising reviewer cost — the v5 reviewer must now review a capability the shared TU has never had.

TRIANGULATION is the hardest of the three: the registration mechanics move OUT of the per-addon TU into a shared one, so the reviewer can no longer compare A8 to A3 as parallel self-contained surfaces — they must trace A8 wrapper -> A1 helper -> sysfs, AND hold the deferred-cascade end-state in mind to judge whether the abstraction is even justified.

The stated 'all three call it' goal is unreachable within this deliverable — it requires a force-push cascade on already-soaked a2-*/a3-* fork branches (lease-push + range-diff + catalog update + per-branch user-confirm under the carve-out). Until step 2 lands, the helper has exactly ONE caller (A8), at which point the indirection has NEGATIVE value: cross-TU indirection serving a single consumer, wrapping a 3-line body (guard + create + warn).

Inverts the dependency direction: A8 (leaf observability addon) would depend on A1 (core primitives) for sysfs mechanics it can trivially inline — an extra manifest-order / build dependency for marginal benefit, and a coupling the addon-recarve campaign worked to REMOVE.

Highest process risk for the lowest substantive change: same estimated_loc (35) as B but spread across a shared TU + a deferred cascade, maximising provenance/review surface while the actual A8 fix (mechanism swap) is identical to A's.

verdicts.winner: A — CONSERVATIVE (clone A3/A2 idiom into dedicated nv-tb-egpu-metrics.{c,h} TU)
verdicts.runner_up: B — IMPROVED (stay inline in nv.c, split attrs_init from metrics_reset, add registered-flag)
verdicts.composite_recommendation: Adopt Variant A outright as the A8 v2 mechanism. Through the coexistence + review-cost lens it dominates: all three coexist cleanly with A3 (disjoint prefixes, same pci-device kobj, flat groups, independent lifecycles, A3 .patch untouched — none breaks A3's attributes), so coexistence does NOT discriminate. Review-cost does, decisively. A is the ONLY variant the queued v5 deep review can triangulate by pure file-against-file diff: lay nv-tb-egpu-metrics.c beside nv-tb-egpu-recover.c:786-884 and nv-tb-egpu-qwd.c:353-455 and the skeleton is line-for-line identical, in the exact dedicated-TU geometry A3/A2/A1 already ship. A adds the least NEW behavioral surface (zero divergence from A3's fail-soft, log level, guards, teardown order; no new nvl struct field; no new module param) and structurally eliminates the dev_groups footgun. The file churn (2 files + 1 Kbuild line) is review-POSITIVE because it reproduces A3's own layout. Note one fact that strengthens A and weakens the others' framing: A8 v1 was never committed (no dev_groups/attr_group in the live tree, task #273 pending), so the 'relocation enlarges the v1->v2 diff' objection is largely moot — there is no committed v1 hunk to diff against; the reviewer reviews the new TU on its merits. GRAFT FROM B (the runner-up), kept minimal so as not to erode A's line-for-line triangulability: (1) take B's nv_tb_egpu_metrics_reset() data/control-plane split ONLY IF the fake-5090 test-infra or A9 concretely needs to drive counter-init independently of sysfs — it is a clean seam with real test value; if no consumer needs it yet, omit it to stay verbatim-A. (2) Do NOT graft B's NV_DBG_ERRORS log-level change — keep A3's NV_DBG_INFO so the three sibling surfaces log identically (a deliberate consistency the v5 pass will reward). (3) Do NOT graft B's tb_egpu_attrs_registered flag — A3 proves it is unnecessary for a flat group (sysfs_remove_group on a never-created flat group is a benign no-op), and adding it diverges from A3 to solve a non-problem. REJECT Variant C unless and until the team firmly commits to completing the deferred A2/A3 cascade conversion in the same campaign: it introduces a brand-new sysfs capability into the A1 shared TU (verified to contain zero sysfs today — only tb_egpu_pcie_* register reads), maximises the v5 reviewer's surface, inverts the addon->core dependency the recarve campaign worked to remove, and otherwise leaves the helper as cross-TU indirection serving a single caller (negative value). C's own author already recommends A as the safe default; concur. Two minor follow-ups for whichever path ships, both doc-only: bump the A8 intent doc to v2 (task #275, invert the dev_groups requirement, record the v1 failure as provenance), and add a one-line comment in metrics_stop noting that sysfs-remove-first ordering is intentional A3-symmetry / vestigial (no per-pdev kfree to race) so a future reader doesn't mistake it for a live race guard. Cosmetic: consider whether tb_egpu_state should be tb_egpu_f40b_state to avoid sitting one ls-line away from A3's existing tb_egpu_recover_state — no collision, pure readability, the author's call.

synthesis.composite_design_summary: A8 v2 = Variant A (CONSERVATIVE) as the base mechanism, with one targeted graft from Variant B. Relocate A8 v1's unchanged surface (enum, module-static atomic metrics struct, nv_tb_egpu_state_str, the three A9-facing hooks, the 5 DEVICE_ATTR_RO show functions, the flat const attribute_group) out of the nv.c hunk into a dedicated new translation unit kernel-open/nvidia/nv-tb-egpu-metrics.{c,h}, mirroring A1/A3's file geometry (new .c + .h + Kbuild edit, all inside one .patch). Swap the empirically-broken pci_driver.driver.dev_groups registration for A3's proven sysfs_create_group / sysfs_remove_group on &nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj, invoked from two new lifecycle helpers tb_egpu_metrics_init(nvl) / tb_egpu_metrics_stop(nvl) wired into nv_pci_probe (beside A2's QWD init at line ~2305) and nv_pci_remove_helper (before A2's QWD stop at line ~2387). GRAFT FROM B: add a small nv_tb_egpu_metrics_reset() helper called at the top of tb_egpu_metrics_init BEFORE sysfs_create_group, so every (re)bind generation starts at state=HEALTHY / counters=0 — this fixes the DaemonSet-pod-restart stale-counter correctness bug that the pure-A and pure-C variants leave open and that the correctness judge flagged as the single decisive item. DECLINE the rest of B: keep A3's NV_DBG_INFO log level (not NV_DBG_ERRORS) for sibling-surface consistency, and do NOT add a per-device tb_egpu_attrs_registered flag (A8's state is module-static and never kfree'd, so sysfs_remove_group on a never-created flat group is a benign no-op; the flag would diverge from A3 to solve a non-problem). REJECT Variant C outright — it injects the first-ever sysfs code into A1's register-read-only shared TU, inverts the addon→core dependency, and strands a single-caller abstraction unless the team commits to a force-push cascade conversion of the already-soaked A2/A3, which is out of scope. The 5 attributes, their names/semantics, the A6 hook wiring, and the A9 hook symbols all carry forward verbatim; only the registration mechanism, file location, and the reset-on-bind behavior change.
synthesis.chosen_design: Variant A (CONSERVATIVE) base + Variant B's metrics-reset graft; Variant C rejected
deltas_from_chosen_variant: GRAFT FROM B: add static void nv_tb_egpu_metrics_reset(void) and call it at the top of tb_egpu_metrics_init() BEFORE sysfs_create_group. It does atomic_set(state, HEALTHY); atomic_set(f40b_fires/recovery_count/recovery_failures, 0); atomic64_set(last_recovery_ns, 0). Rationale: A8's metrics struct is module-lifetime static and is never reset, so on a DaemonSet pod restart / driver unbind-rebind the same global struct is re-exposed against the new device kobj carrying STALE counters from the prior bind generation. This is a real observability-correctness bug under exactly the device-plugin restart scenario this project runs. Variant A (and C) left it open; B alone fixed it. This is the only behavioral change grafted in.
DECLINE B's NV_DBG_ERRORS: keep A3/A2's NV_DBG_INFO log level on sysfs_create_group failure so the three sibling tb_egpu_* surfaces log identically (the v5 deep-review will reward this consistency).

DECLINE B's tb_egpu_attrs_registered per-device flag and B's rc-propagation seam: A8's module-static, never-freed state means there is no per-pdev allocation to race against and sysfs_remove_group on a never-created flat group is a documented benign no-op (A3 deliberately omits such a flag). Keep tb_egpu_metrics_init returning int (returns 0 unconditionally) only for signature symmetry with tb_egpu_recover_init / tb_egpu_qwd_init; the call site uses the (void) cast like its siblings.

FROM A, KEEP the dedicated-TU relocation (nv-tb-egpu-metrics.{c,h} + Kbuild edit) rather than B's stay-inline-in-nv.c. Verified that A1 and A3 patches both create their own .c/.h TU plus a Kbuild line inside a single .patch — so the dedicated-TU layout is the native patch geometry here and lets the v5 reviewer diff nv-tb-egpu-metrics.c 1:1 against nv-tb-egpu-recover.c. The A6 hook call (nv_tb_egpu_f40b_fired();) stays in nv.c's nv_open_device_for_nvlfp_bounded and now resolves via a new #include "nv-tb-egpu-metrics.h" in nv.c instead of a same-TU definition.

files_to_modify.change_summary: Rewrite this single patch file in place (it is NOT yet applied to any soaked tree, so no force-push cascade). The rewritten patch contains FOUR diff sections: (1) NEW file kernel-open/nvidia/nv-tb-egpu-metrics.c (new file mode 100644) holding the relocated, unchanged A8 v1 body — enum nv_tb_egpu_state, the module-static nv_tb_egpu_metrics atomic struct, nv_tb_egpu_state_str(), the three hooks nv_tb_egpu_f40b_fired/_recovery_succeeded/_recovery_failed, the 5 DEVICE_ATTR_RO show fns, static struct attribute *tb_egpu_metrics_attrs[], static const struct attribute_group tb_egpu_metrics_attr_group = { .attrs = tb_egpu_metrics_attrs } (renamed from v1 nv_tb_egpu_attr_group; the v1 nv_tb_egpu_dev_groups[] wrapper is DELETED), plus the new static void nv_tb_egpu_metrics_reset(void) and the two lifecycle helpers tb_egpu_metrics_init/_stop calling sysfs_create_group/sysfs_remove_group on &nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj with the A3 fail-soft idiom (NV_DBG_INFO, if(nvl->pci_dev) guard, reset-before-create). The .c must #include the project's standard nv headers (match nv-tb-egpu-recover.c's include block: nv-linux.h etc.) and #include "nv-tb-egpu-metrics.h". (2) NEW file kernel-open/nvidia/nv-tb-egpu-metrics.h (new file mode 100644) mirroring nv-tb-egpu-recover.h: typedef/forward-declare nv_linux_state_t, declare int tb_egpu_metrics_init(nv_linux_state_t *nvl) + void tb_egpu_metrics_stop(nv_linux_state_t *nvl) + the three A9-facing hooks void nv_tb_egpu_f40b_fired(void)/nv_tb_egpu_recovery_succeeded(void)/nv_tb_egpu_recovery_failed(int permanent). (3) PATCH kernel-open/nvidia/nv.c: add #include "nv-tb-egpu-metrics.h" next to the existing addon includes at nv.c:58-59; REMOVE the entire v1 implementation block (the @@ -1822,+138 @@ insertion: enum/struct/state_str/hooks/show fns/attrs/attr_group/dev_groups[]); KEEP only the single nv_tb_egpu_f40b_fired(); call inside nv_open_device_for_nvlfp_bounded (it now resolves via the include). (4) PATCH kernel-open/nvidia/nv-pci.c: REMOVE the v1 extern const struct attribute_group *nv_tb_egpu_dev_groups[]; block and the .driver.dev_groups = nv_tb_egpu_dev_groups, line from struct nv_pci_driver; ADD #include "nv-tb-egpu-metrics.h" beside the A2/A3 includes at nv-pci.c:30-31; ADD (void)tb_egpu_metrics_init(nvl); in nv_pci_probe immediately after the (void)tb_egpu_qwd_init(nvl); at line ~2305; ADD tb_egpu_metrics_stop(nvl); in nv_pci_remove_helper immediately before tb_egpu_qwd_stop(nvl); at line ~2387. (5) PATCH kernel-open/nvidia/nvidia-sources.Kbuild: add NVIDIA_SOURCES += nvidia/nv-tb-egpu-metrics.c directly below the nv-tb-egpu-recover.c line (Kbuild:16). The patch's leading comment block must be corrected from its current mislabeling (it self-references 'A7' and attributes recovery hooks to 'A8') to the correct A8/A9 numbering, and the 'Registration is via nv_pci_driver.driver.dev_groups' note replaced with the sysfs_create_group description.

Bump to v2 (task #275). Invert the requirement that currently FORBIDS sysfs_create_group: now mandate explicit sysfs_create_group at probe / sysfs_remove_group at remove mirroring A3, and state the dev_groups mechanism MUST NOT be used. Reword the three scenario/requirement passages that attribute lifecycle to dev_groups (the requirement preamble parenthetical, the 'disappear on unbind / without driver-code involvement' THEN-clause, and the telemetry-contract table row 'registered with PCI core at module init via dev_groups') to describe per-device probe-time sysfs_create_group registration; preserve all observable outcomes (5 attrs, names, semantics, appear-on-bind/disappear-on-unbind, A6 increments fires + sets lost-temporary, A9 hook symbols present today). Update the 'Vanilla baseline files' provenance: drop the nv-pci.c dev_groups field edit; describe the new nv-tb-egpu-metrics.{c,h} TU + Kbuild edit + the probe/remove call-site wiring. Add a 'why v2' revision-history note recording the v1 dev_groups failure (compiled + symbols in kallsyms but zero sysfs paths materialised) with root cause: __pci_register_driver (pci-driver.c:1442) does drv->driver.dev_groups = drv->dev_groups, clobbering the v1-set inner field with the NULL outer pci_driver.dev_groups. Cite A7 Test A validation report (line 163 no-show, rec #2 migration) and Test B report (rec #5 boost-priority) as empirical basis. Add a NEW requirement: counters MUST reset to zero / state to healthy on each device bind (covers the rebind-stale-counter fix). Keep all five scope boundaries (no recovery action, no uevents, no force_trigger, no per-device storage, no modification of A3 attrs); confirm the no-per-device-storage boundary wording does not imply per-device counters (the group attaches per-device but backing storage stays module-global single-instance).

new_helpers_to_add: int tb_egpu_metrics_init(nv_linux_state_t *nvl); /* in nv-tb-egpu-metrics.c. Body: if (!nvl) return 0; nv_tb_egpu_metrics_reset(); if (nvl->pci_dev) { int rc = sysfs_create_group(&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj, &tb_egpu_metrics_attr_group); if (rc != 0) NV_DEV_PRINTF(NV_DBG_INFO, NV_STATE_PTR(nvl), \"tb_egpu metrics: sysfs_create_group failed: %d\\n\", rc); } return 0; — mirrors tb_egpu_recover_init signature/return for sibling symmetry; (void)-cast at call site. Use the same NV_DEV_PRINTF nv-state argument idiom A3 uses (derive nv pointer the way nv-tb-egpu-recover.c does at its create site).

void tb_egpu_metrics_stop(nv_linux_state_t *nvl); /* in nv-tb-egpu-metrics.c. Body: if (!nvl) return; if (nvl->pci_dev) sysfs_remove_group(&nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj, &tb_egpu_metrics_attr_group); — sysfs removed first; no kfree (state is module-static). Mirrors tb_egpu_recover_stop / tb_egpu_qwd_stop.

static void nv_tb_egpu_metrics_reset(void); /* GRAFT FROM B. In nv-tb-egpu-metrics.c, file-static. Body: atomic_set(&nv_tb_egpu_metrics.state, NV_TB_EGPU_STATE_HEALTHY); atomic_set(&nv_tb_egpu_metrics.f40b_fires, 0); atomic_set(&nv_tb_egpu_metrics.recovery_count, 0); atomic_set(&nv_tb_egpu_metrics.recovery_failures, 0); atomic64_set(&nv_tb_egpu_metrics.last_recovery_ns, 0); — called at the top of tb_egpu_metrics_init before sysfs_create_group so each bind generation starts clean (fixes rebind stale-counter bug).
implementation_brief.manifest_changes: NONE to the manifest file itself — patches/manifest already has the row 'A8-f40b-sysfs-observability addon - fork:a8-f40b-sysfs-observability' (line 26), and the patch id / filename are unchanged. The new nv-tb-egpu-metrics.c TU is registered via the Kbuild edit carried INSIDE the A8 patch (not via the manifest). Note for the implementer: the manifest source column points at fork branch a8-f40b-sysfs-observability; because A8 v1 was never landed/soaked in /root/open-gpu-kernel-modules (verified: no nv_tb_egpu_* symbols in the applied tree), the v2 rewrite is a normal commit on that fork branch — NOT a force-push cascade. If the fork branch already carries the v1 dev_groups commit, amend/replace it there (single-branch, no downstream a-branch dependents reach into A8's TU, so no carve-out conditions are triggered).
implementation_brief.compile_validation_command: cd /root/nvidia-driver-injector && tools/validate-patchset.sh --fork /root/open-gpu-kernel-modules --kernel "$(uname -r)"   # checks out the vanilla NVIDIA_OPEN_TAG (read from Dockerfile ARG NVIDIA_OPEN_TAG) into a throwaway worktree, composes the full manifest-ordered C+E+A patch set INCLUDING the rewritten A8, and runs `make modules` against /lib/modules/$(uname -r)/build. Per project policy (compile_validation_not_apply_check) a real `make modules` — not `git apply --check` — is the gate; A8 v2 adds a brand-new TU (nv-tb-egpu-metrics.c) so a missing #include would pass --check but fail the compile, exactly the P5 nv-misc.h class of failure this rule guards against.
implementation_brief.commit_message_subject: A8 v2: register f40b sysfs surface via sysfs_create_group (drop broken dev_groups)
intent_doc_revisions: Bump front-matter status/version to v2 (2026-05-30); add explicit version marker consistent with A7's v1.x tracking.
Invert the dev_groups requirement: mandate explicit sysfs_create_group at probe / sysfs_remove_group at remove (A3 pattern); state pci_driver.driver.dev_groups MUST NOT be used.

Reword the three dev_groups-attributed passages (requirement preamble parenthetical, 'disappear on unbind without driver-code involvement' THEN-clause, telemetry-table 'registered with PCI core at module init via dev_groups' row) to describe per-device probe-time sysfs_create_group registration; preserve every observable outcome.

Add a NEW requirement: on each device bind the counters reset to 0 and state to 'healthy' (documents the metrics-reset-on-bind graft; prevents stale counters across DaemonSet pod restarts / unbind-rebind).

Update 'Vanilla baseline files' provenance: drop the nv-pci.c dev_groups edit; describe new nv-tb-egpu-metrics.{c,h} TU + Kbuild edit + probe/remove call-site wiring in nv-pci.c + the nv.c include and f40b hook retention.

Add 'why v2' revision note: v1 dev_groups compiled + symbols in kallsyms but no sysfs paths on disk; root cause __pci_register_driver clobber of the inner .driver.dev_groups with the NULL outer field; cite A7 Test A (line 163, rec #2) and Test B (rec #5).
