Fix the A8 patch comment block's stale numbering (it self-labels 'A7' and attributes recovery hooks to 'A8') so doc and patch agree that A8 is the sysfs surface and A9 is the forthcoming recovery state machine that populates recovery_count/_failures/_last_recovery_ns.

Confirm the 'no per-device storage' scope boundary wording still holds: the attribute group attaches per-device, but the backing atomic struct stays module-global single-instance (single-eGPU posture); note the multi-device aliasing as an explicit, documented assumption rather than a silent one.

risk_callouts: STALE-NUMBERING IN THE SHIPPED PATCH: the current A8 patch file's comment block self-identifies as 'A7' and attributes the recovery hooks to 'A8', while the manifest+tree have A6=bounded-wait-open, A7=bounded-wait-shutdown, A8=this sysfs surface, A9=forthcoming recovery. The implementer MUST correct the comment numbering during the rewrite or the doc/code provenance will lie. This is cosmetic but the v5 deep review will flag it.

SHOW-FUNCTION STATE RESOLUTION (correctness judge MUST-FIX): the 5 v1 show functions read the module-global nv_tb_egpu_metrics struct DIRECTLY (no pci_get_drvdata / nvl deref), so they carry no NULL hazard during a teardown/rebind window and need no if(!nvl) guard. The implementer MUST preserve this direct-global-read form when relocating into the new TU; do NOT 'improve' them to deref drvdata, which would introduce the very NULL-during-teardown race A3 has to guard against.

MULTI-DEVICE COUNTER ALIASING: backing state is module-global, so if a second nvidia-bound PCI device ever exists, both devices' tb_egpu_* attrs alias one counter set (and a second bind would call nv_tb_egpu_metrics_reset, zeroing the first device's counters). Acceptable under the single-eGPU deployment posture and matches v1, but must be an EXPLICIT documented assumption in the v2 intent, not silent. The reset-on-bind graft slightly sharpens this (a 2nd bind resets shared counters) — fine for single-GPU, flagged for the eventual multi-GPU refactor.

RESET-ON-BIND vs A6/A9 IN-FLIGHT STATE: nv_tb_egpu_metrics_reset runs on every probe. On the single-eGPU host this is correct (a fresh bind = fresh generation). But if A9's recovery ever performs an in-driver re-bind cycle as part of recovery, a reset there would zero the very recovery counters A9 just set. Out of scope for A8 (A9 is forthcoming) but the A9 author must be aware the metrics zero on bind — note this in the intent's A9 cross-ref.

KOBJ NON-NULL ASSUMPTION: like A3/A2, the helpers guard if(nvl->pci_dev) but do NOT separately NULL-check &nvl->pci_dev->dev.kobj — relying on the PCI core having initialised the embedded device kobj before probe. Verified-safe and matches A3/A2 deliberate omission; preserve, do not add a spurious kobj check.

COMPILE GATE IS LOAD-BEARING FOR A NEW TU: A8 v2 introduces nv-tb-egpu-metrics.c. A missing #include (e.g. for ktime_get_ns, atomic ops, sysfs_emit, or NV_DEV_PRINTF/NV_STATE_PTR) would pass `git apply --check` but fail `make modules` — exactly the P5 nv-misc.h failure class in project policy. The validate-patchset.sh `make modules` run is mandatory before claiming the series good; do not substitute --check.
open_questions_for_user: The A8 patch comment block currently mislabels itself 'A7' and attributes recovery hooks to 'A8'. I plan to correct it to A8 (this surface) / A9 (forthcoming recovery). Confirm A9 is the canonical id for the forthcoming in-driver recovery state machine that will populate recovery_count/_failures/_last_recovery_ns, so the cross-refs are right.

Reset-on-bind behavior: I'm grafting nv_tb_egpu_metrics_reset() so counters zero on each device bind, fixing stale counters across DaemonSet pod restarts / unbind-rebind. This means a rebind clears f40b_fires history. Confirm that's the desired semantics for your Prometheus/dashboard consumers — the alternative (module-load-lifetime monotonic counters that survive rebind) was v1's implicit behavior. I recommend reset-on-bind because the surface is per-device-bind-scoped and the correctness review flagged surviving stale counters as a bug, but it is a contract choice worth your sign-off.

Whether to give A8 a per-feature kill-switch module param (NVreg_TbEgpuMetricsEnable) for parity with A2's NVreg_TbEgpuQwdEnable and A3's NVreg_TbEgpuRecoverEnable. v1 had none and I am NOT adding one (keeps v2 a minimal mechanism-swap). If you want sibling parity / an off-switch for the observability surface, that's a small additive follow-on — confirm you do NOT want it in this change.

testBPlan.test_question: Primary (candidate a, sharpened): "When rmmod reaches nv_shutdown_adapter with NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED still set (persistence engaged) on a chip whose C5 sink was ALREADY set by a prior A6 open-path fire (cycle-2 open on the F40-precondition substrate), does rm_disable_adapter / rm_shutdown_adapter FAST-FAIL on the pre-existing C5 sink (A7 wrapper logs 'completed within budget' for both, matching the patch intent's original prediction) — OR does rm_shutdown_adapter STILL time out structurally at 200 ms exactly like Test A despite the sink being set?" Secondary (candidate b): "Does persistence-engaged rmmod traverse A7's wrap at all, and does it hit A7's timeout branch differently from the non-persistence Test B path (which skipped nv_shutdown_adapter entirely via the nv_pci_remove_helper NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED short-circuit)?" The two collapse into one run: persistence keeps NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED set, which is precisely what forces rmmod down the nv_shutdown_adapter path Test B never exercised, so A7's wrap is reached and we can finally read whether the C5 sink fast-fails the RM calls or whether the structural 200 ms hang is sink-independent.
testBPlan.delta_vs_test_b: Insert one step — `nvidia-smi -pm 1` (persistence engage) — into Phase 1 AFTER `modprobe --ignore-install nvidia` and BEFORE the Phase 2 cycle-1 open, so NV_FLAG_PERSISTENT_SW_STATE keeps NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED set across LAST-CLOSE and the Phase 3 rmmod consequently calls nv_shutdown_adapter (reaching A7's wrap) instead of short-circuiting through nv_pci_remove_helper as Test B did; everything else in the F40-precondition recipe + cycle-1/cycle-2 + rmmod sequence is identical to Test B.

expected_outcomes.variant: Variant A — RM fast-fails on the pre-set C5 sink (patch-intent's original prediction confirmed)
expected_outcomes.mechanism: A6 fired on the Phase 2 cycle-2 open and set the C5 sink (PDB_PROP_GPU_IS_LOST) via rm_cleanup_gpu_lost_state. Persistence kept NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED set, so Phase 3 rmmod's nv_pci_remove_helper calls nv_shutdown_adapter, which calls rm_disable_adapter then rm_shutdown_adapter. RM closed code checks the sink at a sink-aware gate, observes GPU-already-lost, and returns immediately WITHOUT issuing the chip-touching MMIO that hangs. A7's wrapper sees both calls return inside the 200 ms budget.
expected_outcomes.observability: journalctl -k shows: 'rm_disable_adapter scheduled' + 'rm_disable_adapter completed within budget' THEN 'rm_shutdown_adapter scheduled' + 'rm_shutdown_adapter completed within budget' (TWO 'completed within budget' lines, NO 'timed out' line), followed by 'nvidia-nvlink: Unregistered Nvlink Core'. rmmod returns 0 in well under 1 s. Distinct from both Test A (which had a timeout line) and Test B (which had ZERO F40b lines).

expected_outcomes.variant: Variant B — RM still hangs structurally; A7 times out on rm_shutdown_adapter just like Test A (sink-independent structural hang)
expected_outcomes.mechanism: Either A6 did NOT fire/set the sink on the cycle-2 open (chip stayed responsive that cycle), or the C5 sink does NOT gate the specific chip-touching MMIO inside rm_shutdown_adapter. rmmod calls nv_shutdown_adapter; rm_disable_adapter completes within budget (chip responsive to that call, as in Test A); rm_shutdown_adapter issues its MMIO, the chip withholds completion, and A7's bounded worker times out at 200 ms, sets the C5 sink, and returns. nv_shutdown_adapter finishes host-side teardown; rmmod returns 0. This would prove the rm_shutdown_adapter hang is structural and sink-independent — refuting the patch intent's 'RM observes sink and fast-fails' prediction even when the sink is demonstrably pre-set.
expected_outcomes.observability: journalctl -k shows the byte-identical Test A signature: 'rm_disable_adapter completed within budget' THEN 'rm_shutdown_adapter timed out after 200 ms — declaring GPU lost (detector_class=3 DETECTOR_AER_FATAL); worker leaked', then 'nvidia-nvlink: Unregistered Nvlink Core'. Wall-clock for rmmod ~1 s (one 200 ms timeout). The presence of the 'timed out' line on a chip that A6 already sink-set in Phase 2 is the discriminator.

expected_outcomes.variant: Variant C — chained A6→A7 wedge escapes both wrappers (failure-of-containment, low-probability but must be named)
expected_outcomes.mechanism: On a doubly-stressed substrate (A6 already fired + persistence-engaged teardown), the rm_shutdown_adapter MMIO blocks the CPU and the kernel deadlocks before A7's bounded worker's timeout can be observed (the same AER-vs-deadlock race that wedged 11/12 open-path attempts in F40 Test B v2/VERIFY/TBv2-n2). A7's worker-hop is designed to defeat exactly this by moving the blocking MMIO off the syscall thread, but it has only been validated on singly-stressed (healthy or close-path) chips, never on an A6-pre-fired substrate.
expected_outcomes.observability: journalctl -k goes SILENT after 'rm_shutdown_adapter scheduled to bounded worker' with NO matching 'completed' or 'timed out' line within ~5 s; systemd/k3s timers stop; the rmmod process is stuck in D-state. This is the abort trigger and the reboot-recovery case.

procedure.phase: Phase 0 — pre-flight baseline (NEW vs Test B; mandatory because chip just rebooted)
procedure.action: Capture pre-state PASSIVELY: BAR1 size from sysfs FIRST (never nvidia-smi first on a post-replug chip per no-rpc-observability-on-broken-bar1), then module refcount, uptime, load, and the A7 timeout param. Record T0 for journalctl windowing.
procedure.command: T0=$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S'); echo "T0=$T0"; sed -n '2p' /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:04:00.0/resource; grep -E '^nvidia' /proc/modules; cat /sys/module/nvidia/parameters/NVreg_TbEgpuShutdownTimeoutMs; uptime
procedure.expected: BAR1 second resource line spans 32 GiB (0x...800000000 range = 0x800000000 bytes); nvidia + nvidia_uvm loaded; NVreg_TbEgpuShutdownTimeoutMs=200; host alive. Confirms healthy substrate and A7 active before we touch the chip.
procedure.safety_concern: none — all reads are passive sysfs/procfs. Do NOT run nvidia-smi here; BAR1 must be confirmed 32 GiB by sysfs before any RPC-issuing tool touches the chip.

procedure.action: Graceful host-side teardown of the running injector driver via the pod's uninstall subcommand (this fires A7 once during the healthy unload — sink set or timeout, host-side cleanup completes, modules unload). This is the same Phase 1 step 1 as Test B.
procedure.command: POD=$(kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app=nvidia-driver-injector -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}'); echo "pod=$POD"; kubectl exec -n kube-system "$POD" -- /entrypoint.sh uninstall
procedure.expected: entrypoint prints 'node-label removed / rmmod nvidia_uvm / rmmod nvidia / uninstall ok'; /proc/modules clean afterward. A7 fires once here (expected per Test A — rm_shutdown_adapter timeout on this healthy unload).
procedure.safety_concern: This rmmod itself exercises A7 on a healthy chip; per Test A n=2 this is host-safe, but it IS a chip-touch and the first wedge opportunity. If host goes silent here, abort and reboot.

procedure.action: TB deauthorize then reauthorize to drive the chip into broken-BAR1 (F41), establishing the userspace-recovered substrate F40-open requires.
procedure.expected: Device re-enumerates; BAR1 now reports broken (256 MiB) on sysfs. ~6 s wall-clock total.
procedure.safety_concern: TB reauth is a surprise-removal-adjacent operation; with the driver already unloaded (step 2) there is no consumer holding the device, so the surprise-removal wedge class does not apply here. Safe given module-unloaded ordering.

procedure.action: Clear the memory-decode bit (fix-bar1 safety precondition), restore BAR1 to 32 GiB via fix-bar1.sh, then clear the driver_override fix-bar1 leaves at 'none'.
procedure.expected: fix-bar1.sh writes ReBAR CTRL=0x00000f21, pciehp slot-cycles, BAR1 back to 32 GiB in ~7 s; driver_override now empty. State captures land in /var/log/fix-bar1-<ts>/.
procedure.safety_concern: none beyond the documented fix-bar1 behaviour; this is the proven recovery path from F40 catalog, exercised cleanly in Test B.

procedure.action: Load the patched module manually (host policy has 'install nvidia /bin/false', so --ignore-install is required; NOT via the pod, for control parity with Test B).
procedure.expected: nvidia.ko binds to 0000:04:00.0; /dev/nvidia0 + /dev/nvidiactl present; nvidia-nvlink core init line in journal; 'external GPU detected' (E1) line. BAR1 still 32 GiB.
procedure.safety_concern: modprobe-spin watch begins here: Test B's Phase 4 saw modprobe spin at 99.6% CPU for 7+ min AFTER nvidia.ko was fully loaded. If modprobe does not return within ~30 s, jump to abort/recovery — do not SIGKILL-and-wait (Test B showed SIGKILL did not terminate it).

procedure.phase: Phase 1 — PERSISTENCE ENGAGE (THE DELTA from Test B)
procedure.action: Engage persistence so NV_FLAG_PERSISTENT_SW_STATE is set and NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED stays set across LAST-CLOSE — this is what forces the Phase 3 rmmod down the nv_shutdown_adapter path that reaches A7's wrap. BAR1 was already confirmed 32 GiB at step 4, so this nvidia-smi is safe to run.
procedure.expected: 'Persistence mode is already Enabled' or 'Enabled for GPU 0000:04:00.0'; rc=0. A6 open-path wrap fires here on the persistence-engage open (per Test A side-observation) and completes within budget. Persistence now engaged.
procedure.safety_concern: nvidia-smi issues RPC/MMIO. SAFE here ONLY because BAR1 was passively confirmed 32 GiB at step 4 and the chip is freshly recovered. If step 4's BAR1 had been broken this call could wedge per no-rpc-observability-on-broken-bar1 — re-confirm 32 GiB before running if any doubt.

procedure.phase: Phase 2 — cycle 1 (clean) then cycle 2 (A6 may fire)
procedure.action: Cycle 1: a clean chip-touching open/close. With persistence engaged, LAST-CLOSE does NOT run nv_stop_device teardown (the Test B close-path A7 fire is suppressed by design — that is the point).
procedure.expected: Clean single-GPU listing (e.g. 'GPU 0: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090'); rc=0; <1 s. Crucially NO close-path A7 fire in the journal (persistence prevents nv_stop_device), unlike Test B's cycle-1 which DID fire A7 on LAST-CLOSE.
procedure.safety_concern: RPC/MMIO call; safe on the confirmed-32GiB substrate. The absence of an A7 close-path fire here (vs Test B) is itself a checkpoint that persistence took effect — if A7 fires on this LAST-CLOSE, persistence did NOT engage and the run is invalid (treat as soft-abort, see abort criteria).

procedure.phase: Phase 2 — cycle 2 (A6 fires, sets C5 sink)
procedure.action: Cycle 2: open /dev/nvidia0 directly. On the F40-precondition substrate this hits the F40-open arm; A6's bounded worker times out at 200 ms, sets the C5 sink, returns -EIO. This is what pre-sets the sink that Phase 3 will test rm_shutdown_adapter against.
procedure.expected: bash reports 'Input/output error', rc=1. Journal shows 'open scheduled to bounded worker' then 'open timed out after 200 ms — declaring GPU lost'. C5 sink now set. (If A6 instead completes within budget, the chip stayed responsive and we are in a sink-NOT-set sub-case — note it; rmmod will then test the structural-hang question directly.)
procedure.safety_concern: This is the canonical F40-open wedge trigger. A6 is validated to contain it (n=2 F40B-TEST), but it is a wedge-class operation: if the host goes silent here instead of returning -EIO within ~1 s, the A6 worker-hop lost the AER-vs-deadlock race — abort and reboot.

procedure.phase: Phase 3 — Test B-prime trigger (rmmod WITH NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED set)
procedure.action: rmmod the module. Because persistence kept NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED set, nv_pci_remove_helper now CALLS nv_shutdown_adapter (unlike Test B's short-circuit), reaching A7's rm_disable_adapter + rm_shutdown_adapter wraps on a chip whose sink A6 set in step 8. Run under timeout so a wedge in the rmmod call itself is bounded on the harness side. Start a fresh journal window marker first.
procedure.command: T3=$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S'); echo "T3=$T3"; timeout 20 rmmod nvidia; echo "rmmod rc=$?"
procedure.expected: VARIANT A: two 'completed within budget' lines (disable + shutdown), no timeout, rmmod rc=0 in <1 s. VARIANT B: 'rm_disable_adapter completed within budget' + 'rm_shutdown_adapter timed out after 200 ms', rmmod rc=0 in ~1 s (Test A signature). Either is host-safe and answers the test question. timeout-124 rc = harness-side bound hit = Variant C wedge.
procedure.safety_concern: PRIMARY measurement step and primary wedge opportunity (Variant C). The bash-side `timeout 20` bounds the harness but does NOT bound the kernel — if the kernel deadlocks, rmmod will be stuck in D-state and timeout cannot kill it. A silent journal after 'rm_shutdown_adapter scheduled' is the abort signal.

procedure.action: Capture the F40b journal lines for the Phase 3 window and classify the variant. Passive read only.
procedure.expected: The exact F40b line sequence that discriminates Variant A (two 'completed within budget') from Variant B ('rm_shutdown_adapter timed out after 200 ms') from Test B (zero F40b lines — which MUST NOT occur here, since that would mean nv_shutdown_adapter was skipped and persistence failed to hold NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED).
procedure.safety_concern: none — passive journalctl read.

procedure.action: Confirm modules clean, host alive, BAR1 intact (passive). Then respawn the injector pod ONLY IF the host is healthy. Watch for the Test B Phase 4 modprobe-spin recurrence.
procedure.command: grep -E '^nvidia' /proc/modules || echo 'modules clean'; sed -n '2p' /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:04:00.0/resource; uptime; kubectl delete pod -n kube-system "$POD"
procedure.expected: /proc/modules clean; BAR1 32 GiB; host alive. DaemonSet respawns a new pod; new pod reaches READY in ~50 s with a clean modprobe (NO 99.6% CPU spin). nvidia-smi works after respawn.
procedure.safety_concern: Test B's UNRESOLVED Phase 4 modprobe spin (99.6% CPU, 7+ min, SIGKILL-immune) may recur. If the respawned pod's modprobe spins, do NOT chase it — the test data (steps 9-10) is already captured; elect reboot. Capture PID + /proc/PID/stack + a 5 s perf sample first IF the host is otherwise responsive.

procedure.action: After respawn READY, verify GPU is healthy. BAR1 was confirmed 32 GiB at step 11, so nvidia-smi is safe.
procedure.command: kubectl wait --for=condition=ready pod -n kube-system -l app=nvidia-driver-injector --timeout=120s; nvidia-smi --query-gpu=persistence_mode,temperature.gpu,power.draw,pstate --format=csv,noheader
procedure.expected: Pod READY; nvidia-smi returns 'Enabled, ~36 C, ~22-64 W, P8/P0'. Clean recovery, run complete.
procedure.safety_concern: nvidia-smi safe only after step-11 sysfs BAR1 re-confirmation. If respawn did not reach READY (modprobe spin), skip this and go to reboot recovery.

safety_considerations: Chip just rebooted (22 min uptime confirmed) and is in MAXIMALLY HEALTHY state — full 32 GiB BAR1, persistence-clean, no prior F40 fire this boot. This is the BEST-case substrate; any wedge observed here is a floor, not a worst-case. Do not over-generalize a clean result to chronically-stressed states.

This is a SINGLE chip-touch cycle (n=1). Unlike Test A's n=2 setup there is no soak/reload between repetitions. The result is a single data point; per reliability methodology n>=3 is needed to resolve structural-vs-circumstantial, so frame the outcome as provisional and DO NOT claim 'A7 fast-fails on sink' (or the converse) as validated from this one run.

Test B's Phase 4 modprobe spin (99.6% CPU, R-state, 7+ min, SIGKILL-immune, nvidia-smi 'couldn't communicate') is UNRESOLVED and may recur at step 5 (manual modprobe) or step 11 (pod respawn). Be ready for it: it does NOT invalidate the steps-9/10 measurement, and the correct response is reboot, not chasing the spin. Capture PID/stack/perf only if the host is otherwise responsive.

nvidia-smi (and any RPC/MMIO-issuing observability) WILL WEDGE the host if BAR1 is broken (256 MiB) — per feedback_no_rpc_observability_on_broken_bar1 (cost 2 reboots on 2026-05-28). Therefore EVERY nvidia-smi in this plan (steps 6, 7, 12) is gated on a PRIOR passive sysfs BAR1=32 GiB confirmation (steps 4/11). First check after any chip-state change is ALWAYS the sysfs BAR1 read, never nvidia-smi.

Do NOT chain Test B-prime runs without a reboot between them. The substrate accumulates state across destructive teardowns (F40 'Open questions': cumulative state from multiple destructive teardowns weakens the chip's response capacity — Run 1 survived, Run 2 wedged on the same boot). Each run must start from a fresh reboot to keep the single-variable (persistence engaged) clean.

Step 9 rmmod is the primary Variant-C wedge opportunity: A7's worker-hop has only ever been validated on singly-stressed chips (healthy in Test A, close-path in Test B), NEVER on an A6-pre-fired doubly-stressed substrate. The AER-vs-deadlock race that wedged 11/12 open-path attempts could in principle beat A7's bounded worker here. The bash-side `timeout` bounds the harness but cannot kill a D-state rmmod stuck on blocked MMIO — treat journal-silence-after-'scheduled' as the kernel-deadlock signal.
Step 8 (cycle-2 open) is itself a canonical F40-open wedge trigger; A6 is the only thing containing it. It is validated n=2 but is genuinely a wedge-class operation on this exact substrate.

Passive-observability-first throughout: prefer journalctl -k, sysfs reads, /proc/modules over any active probe; per feedback_observability_perturbs_bug, active observability (bpftrace, CUDA_LAUNCH_BLOCKING) materially changes the failure mode — this plan deliberately uses ONLY the in-driver F40b kernel-log markers (no bpftrace) as Test A/B did.

If persistence fails to engage (step 6 rc!=0, or an A7 close-path fire appears at step 7's LAST-CLOSE), the entire delta-from-Test-B is void — the run would degenerate into a Test B re-run (rmmod short-circuits nv_shutdown_adapter). Detect this at step 7 and soft-abort rather than collect invalid data.

Forensics discipline: set T0/T3 journal markers (steps 1, 9) and preserve the step-10 journal capture + any fix-bar1 state dumps to /var/log/mission-1-archaeology/test-B-prime-<date>/ before any reboot, so an n=1 wedge is not data-lost (mirrors Test B's preservation pattern).
abort_criteria: Kernel journal goes SILENT after 'tb_egpu [F40b]: rm_shutdown_adapter scheduled to bounded worker' (step 9) or after 'open scheduled to bounded worker' (step 8) with NO matching 'completed within budget' / 'timed out after 200 ms' line within ~5 s — this is the Variant-C kernel-deadlock signature; stop, preserve forensics if the host is still partially responsive, reboot.

`timeout 20 rmmod nvidia` (step 9) returns exit code 124 (harness timeout hit) OR the rmmod process is observed in uninterruptible D-state — the rmmod is wedged on blocked MMIO that the bash-side timeout cannot kill; stop and reboot.

Modprobe spin recurs (step 5 or step 11): a modprobe process at ~99.6% CPU in R-state that does not return within ~30 s and is SIGKILL-immune (per Test B Phase 4). Do not chase to root cause; capture PID/stack/perf only if host is responsive, then reboot. Measurement from steps 9-10 is already captured and remains valid.

Persistence did not actually engage: step 6 nvidia-smi -pm 1 returns non-zero, OR an A7 close-path fire ('rm_shutdown_adapter ...') appears in the journal during step 7's nvidia-smi -L LAST-CLOSE. This means NV_FLAG_INITIALIZED would be cleared and the run degenerates to a Test B re-run — soft-abort (the test question cannot be answered) and reboot before retrying.

Any passive sysfs BAR1 read (steps 1, 4, 11) reports a size other than 32 GiB at a point where 32 GiB is expected, AND a subsequent step would issue nvidia-smi/RPC — do NOT run the RPC tool; stop and reboot to avoid the broken-BAR1 wedge.
systemd/k3s timers or other unrelated subsystems stop emitting in journalctl (kernel-wide scheduler wedge symptom from F40), regardless of which step — immediate reboot.
testBPlan.recovery_path_if_wedged: Recovery from ANY wedge is a host reboot — explicitly, per memory feedback_no_rpc_observability_on_broken_bar1 and the F40 catalog's operational guidance, there is NO in-driver recovery for these wedge classes yet (A9 is draft-only). The chip substrate is structurally fine (BAR1 stays 32 GiB through the wedge in all observed cases); only the kernel/userspace is wedged, so a clean reboot fully restores the pre-injector baseline and the DaemonSet re-binds on next boot. Before rebooting, IF the host is still partially responsive: preserve `journalctl -k --since $T0` and any `/var/log/fix-bar1-*` captures into `/var/log/mission-1-archaeology/test-B-prime-<date>/` so the n=1 result survives. Do NOT attempt the userspace rmmod→TB-recycle→fix-bar1→modprobe recovery loop to avoid a reboot — that loop is for clean F40-recovery between healthy runs, not for un-wedging a deadlocked kernel, and Test B already showed SIGKILL on a spun modprobe does not work. Do NOT run nvidia-smi or any RPC tool to 'check on' a suspected-wedged chip — passive reads only, then reboot.

flagged_steps.concern: Step 6 (nvidia-smi -pm 1) is the load-bearing DELTA, but the plan assumes a clean, persistence-OFF substrate established by the manual modprobe at step 5. On the LIVE host I verified the running pod (nvidia-driver-injector-zz4dp, restarts=1, up 10h — this is the SAME pod that wedged in Test B Phase 4) already has the driver loaded with persistence engaged, and there are already 24 F40b 'open completed within budget' fires THIS boot. The plan's premise of 'persistence-clean, no prior F40 fire this boot, 22 min uptime' is FALSE against the actual machine (34 min uptime, persistence active, prior fires). If steps 1-5 teardown/reload run first this resolves, but the operator must NOT skip them thinking the substrate is clean, and step 6 treating 'Persistence mode is already Enabled' as success cannot distinguish 'we engaged it' from 'it was already engaged from a stale session' — defeating the single-variable cleanliness claimed in safety_considerations #1.
flagged_steps.mitigation: REBOOT before executing — the host is not in the premised state right now. After reboot, confirm at Phase 0 that persistence is OFF and zero pre-existing F40b shutdown-path fires this boot, so step 6 is provably the variable introduced. Treat a pre-existing 'already Enabled' at step 6 as a soft-abort signal, not success.

flagged_steps.concern: Phase 0 BAR1 verification uses raw 'sed -n 2p' + eyeball of a hex range, NOT the strtonum/arithmetic size computation the no-rpc-observability-on-broken-bar1 memory mandates as the first post-state-change check. On the current healthy chip line 2 happens to be the 32 GiB BAR1, but BAR ordering in /sys/.../resource is NOT guaranteed stable across a TB re-enumeration / fix-bar1 slot-cycle. After step-3 deauth/reauth and step-4 fix-bar1 the kernel reassigns resources and the 32 GiB region could land on a different line; line 1 is 64 MiB and line 4 is 32 MiB. A hardcoded line-2 read could silently pass a broken-BAR1 as healthy and green-light the nvidia-smi at step 6/7 onto a broken-BAR1 chip — the exact 2-reboot wedge from 2026-05-28.
flagged_steps.mitigation: Replace every 'sed -n 2p' BAR1 check (steps 1/4/11) with a position-independent scan that asserts a >=32 GiB region exists for the prefetchable BAR, using bash $((0x..)) arithmetic (mawk has no strtonum in containers per memory). Never trust a fixed line index across a re-enumeration.

flagged_steps.concern: The modprobe-spin abort path is contradictory and under-bounded. Step 5 says 'do not SIGKILL-and-wait (Test B showed SIGKILL did not terminate it)' and abort criteria say 'reboot,' but the plan gives no way to DISTINGUISH the benign loading case from the Test-B pathological spin (99.6% CPU, R-state, SIGKILL-immune) without waiting ~30s, and provides no `timeout` wrapper on the modprobe. During that wait a process pins a core; if the spinning modprobe holds the global RM lock, subsequent steps would deadlock. The plan treats a step-5 spin as reboot-recoverable but never says to passively confirm BAR1 before that reboot.
flagged_steps.mitigation: Wrap as 'timeout 30 modprobe --ignore-install nvidia'; on timeout-124 OR observed R-state-at-99%-CPU, do NOT proceed and do NOT SIGKILL — go straight to forensic-capture-then-reboot. A step-5 spin means the run never starts, so fail fast.

flagged_steps.concern: Variant C (kernel-deadlock escapes A7) is correctly NAMED as at-risk, but the only containment is the bash-side 'timeout 20 rmmod' which the plan itself admits cannot kill a D-state rmmod. So the single most dangerous step's only real safety mechanism is the operator noticing journal silence and manually rebooting — yet journalctl itself may stop if the scheduler wedges. There is no independent armed watchdog. A7's worker-hop has NEVER been validated on the A6-pre-fired + persistence doubly-stressed substrate this step creates (n=0 prior evidence) on the same hardware where 11/12 open-path attempts wedged.
flagged_steps.mitigation: Before step 9 arm an independent out-of-band recovery: enable sysrq (echo 1 > /proc/sys/kernel/sysrq), pre-position a reboot in an already-forked shell, fsync-tee 'journalctl -kf' to disk so the last pre-wedge lines survive a hard reset, and arm the kernel hung-task detector / hardware watchdog if available so a true scheduler wedge auto-reboots rather than hanging indefinitely.

flagged_steps.concern: Step 11 deletes the running pod to force a DaemonSet respawn — exactly where Test B's UNRESOLVED modprobe-spin occurred. The measurement (steps 9-10) is already captured by step 10 BEFORE step 11 runs, so step 11 introduces a fresh known-pathological failure opportunity AFTER the science is done, for zero measurement benefit. It is pure recovery convenience that re-exposes the host to the one unresolved wedge in this area. I also verified the device-plugin DaemonSet is currently absent (only the injector DS exists), so the readiness-gating protection may differ from assumed.
flagged_steps.mitigation: Make step 11 optional and defer-by-default: after step 10 capture, prefer leaving modules cleanly unloaded and rebooting to restore the injector rather than gambling on the respawn modprobe-spin. If respawn IS attempted, gate it on 'host fully healthy + forensics already preserved' and pre-accept that a spin = immediate reboot with zero debugging time spent.

flagged_steps.concern: Step 8 deliberately triggers the A6 open-path timeout to pre-set the C5 sink. If A6 instead completes-within-budget (chip stayed responsive), the ENTIRE Test B-prime premise collapses — there is no pre-set sink for step 9 to test against — yet the plan proceeds to step 9 anyway and would mislabel a structural-hang as meaningful when the independent variable was never established. Silent-invalid-data risk that wastes the single reboot-gated n=1 run (not a host-safety risk).
flagged_steps.mitigation: Add a hard checkpoint after step 8: confirm the 'open timed out ... declaring GPU lost' line is present in the journal BEFORE proceeding to step 9. If A6 completed-within-budget (no sink set), soft-abort — the run cannot answer the test question this cycle.

missing_safety_steps: No pre-flight verification that the host is actually in the claimed clean state. The plan asserts '22 min uptime, persistence-clean, no prior F40 fire this boot' as a safety_consideration but has NO step that VERIFIES it. I verified on the live host all three are currently false (34 min uptime, persistence engaged via a 10h-old pod that is the same zz4dp pod from Test B's wedge, 24 prior F40b fires this boot). A mandatory Phase -1 'verify clean substrate or reboot first' gate is missing.

No independent/out-of-band deadlock watchdog for step 9. The only Variant-C containment is the operator watching journalctl, which itself dies if the scheduler wedges. Missing: sysrq enablement, hardware/software watchdog arming, or an fsync'd journal tee so the last pre-wedge lines survive a hard reset (the freeze-risk methodology memory calls for fsync'd progress markers precisely for this).

No explicit bound (via `timeout`) on how long to tolerate the step-5 / step-11 modprobe spin before electing reboot. The plan says '~30 s' in prose but does not wrap modprobe, so a SIGKILL-immune spin has no hard stop.

No passive confirmation that the C5 sink is actually SET between step 8 (A6 fire) and step 9 (rmmod). The experiment hinges on it; A8 v1 sysfs failed to materialise, so a journalctl grep for the step-8 'timed out' line is the only available proxy and should be a hard gate before step 9.

No statement of what to do if device 0000:04:00.0 disappears or re-enumerates at a different BDF after the TB deauth/reauth (step 3). Every subsequent hardcoded '04:00.0' / '0-1' path would break and fix-bar1 could operate on a stale/absent device.
recommended_mitigations: REBOOT FIRST. The host is not in the plan's premised clean state (verified: persistence engaged, 24 prior F40b fires this boot, running pod is the same one that wedged in Test B Phase 4). Running now would conflate the single 'persistence' variable.

Replace all hardcoded 'sed -n 2p' BAR1 reads (steps 1/4/11) with a position-independent scan asserting a >=32 GiB prefetchable region exists, using bash $((0x..)) arithmetic (mawk has no strtonum in containers); gate every nvidia-smi (steps 6/7/12) on this computed check, never on a fixed line index.

Arm out-of-band recovery BEFORE step 9: enable sysrq, pre-position a reboot in an already-forked shell, fsync-tee 'journalctl -kf' to /var/log/mission-1-archaeology/test-B-prime-<date>/ so last lines survive a hard reset, and arm the kernel hung-task/watchdog if available.
Wrap modprobe (step 5) and the respawn (step 11) in `timeout 30`; on timeout or 99.6%-CPU R-state, go straight to forensic-capture-then-reboot — do not SIGKILL, do not nurse it.

Make step 11 (pod delete/respawn) optional and defer-by-default: measurement is complete after step 10, so prefer leaving modules cleanly unloaded and rebooting to restore the injector rather than re-exposing the host to the unresolved respawn-modprobe-spin.

Add hard go/no-go checkpoints: after step 7 confirm NO close-path A7 fire (proves persistence engaged); after step 8 confirm the A6 'timed out — declaring GPU lost' line IS present (proves the C5 sink was set). If either fails, soft-abort — do not spend the n=1 reboot budget on invalid data.

Accept this is genuinely n=1 on a never-before-tested doubly-stressed (A6-pre-fired + persistence) substrate where A7's worker-hop has zero prior validation. Frame ALL outcomes as provisional per the n>=3 reliability methodology; do not promote any A7 v1.2 conclusion from this single run.
testBSafety.abort_criteria_review: The abort criteria are unusually thorough and well-grounded in prior forensics — they correctly identify the three real kill-signals (journal-silence-after-'scheduled', timeout-124/D-state rmmod, modprobe-spin), the soft-abort for persistence-not-engaged, and they end every path in reboot, the only validated recovery for these wedge classes. Two gaps: (1) the criteria are entirely PASSIVE/observational — they rely on the operator actively watching at the moment of wedge; there is no armed out-of-band mechanism (sysrq, watchdog, fsync'd journal tee) to act when the very kernel path being watched is what wedges, so a true scheduler-wide wedge could leave the host hung with no auto-recovery and the last evidence lost. (2) The '~5 s' window for declaring journal-silence a deadlock is a judgement call with no instrumented timer; combined with the absent `timeout` wrapper on the modprobe steps, the criteria depend on operator stopwatch discipline. The recovery_path correctly forbids running nvidia-smi to 'check on' a suspected-wedged chip and forbids the userspace recovery loop — sound and matching the memories. Net: directionally excellent but must be made ACTIVE (armed watchdog + bounded timeouts) rather than purely observational before it is safe to run even briefly unattended.
testBSafety.recovery_path_review: The recovery path (host reboot for any wedge; no in-driver recovery since A9 is draft-only) is correct and consistent with the no-rpc-observability memory and F40-catalog guidance. It correctly preserves forensics to /var/log/mission-1-archaeology/test-B-prime-<date>/ before reboot IF the host is partially responsive, correctly forbids the userspace rmmod->TB-recycle->fix-bar1->modprobe loop (that loop is for clean F40-recovery between healthy runs, not for un-wedging a deadlocked kernel), and forbids nvidia-smi on a suspected-wedged chip. The chip-substrate-stays-healthy (BAR1 32 GiB through the wedge) assumption is well-supported by Test A/B evidence. Weakness: the path assumes the host will be 'partially responsive' enough to run forensic preservation before reboot — but a true kernel scheduler wedge (the F40 kernel-wide symptom the plan itself names) may make even passive journalctl/sed unrunnable, in which case the fsync'd-tee-started-BEFORE-step-9 mitigation is the ONLY way the n=1 data survives. Without that pre-armed tee, a hard wedge = total data loss, which for a reboot-gated single-shot run is the worst outcome. Sound but contingent on a 'partially responsive' host that the plan's own worst case (Variant C scheduler wedge) may not provide."
